title: Networking
description: Networking from scratch (beginner rework in progress): 17 sub-topics from layering and the physical layer through IP, subnetting, routing, TCP/UDP, TLS, DNS, wireless and modern transport. Beginner-first readings, MCQ practice, and diagrams; tiered easy to hard.

subtopics.title: Models, Layering & the Shape of the Problem

lessons.title: Why We Split Networking Into Layers
lessons.content:
You use the internet every day, but what is actually happening? At its heart, a **network** is just computers sending each other messages. A **message** is any chunk of information — a web page, a chat, a video. Sending it sounds simple, but the job is huge.

Think about everything that must go right for one message to arrive. The sending computer has to **find** the other machine among billions. It has to **pick a path** to reach it, often through many machines in between. Wires and Wi-Fi are imperfect, so some data gets garbled or lost — something must **catch and fix errors**. And the bytes that arrive are meaningless until the receiving program can **make sense** of them.

That is far too much to solve in one giant tangle. So engineers do what you would do with any big job: break it into smaller jobs. We stack the jobs in **layers**, where each layer handles one job and only talks to the layers directly above and below it — like floors in a building, or stations on an assembly line. The floor below hands work up; this floor does its one task; it hands the result to the floor above.

The payoff is huge. Because each layer only promises a simple service to its neighbor, one team can rebuild a single layer — say, invent faster Wi-Fi — without redesigning the whole internet. 

**Recap:** A network is computers exchanging messages. The work is big, so we split it into layers, each doing one job and trusting its neighbors — which lets any one piece change without breaking the rest.

items.prompt: In the simplest terms, what is a computer network?
choices: A single very powerful computer
Computers sending each other messages
A type of cable used in offices
A program that speeds up your screen
items.explanation: Strip away the jargon and a network is just computers exchanging messages with each other. Everything else (wires, Wi-Fi, programs) exists to make that message exchange work.

items.prompt: Why do we split the work of networking into layers instead of solving it all in one piece?
choices: Because computers can only run one program at a time
Because the job is huge, so we break it into smaller jobs that are each easier to build and change
Because layers make messages travel faster than light
Because the law requires exactly seven layers
items.explanation: Layering is just good engineering: a huge job (find the machine, pick a path, fix errors, interpret bytes) is broken into smaller single-purpose jobs. Speed and legal rules have nothing to do with it.

items.prompt: What is the single biggest benefit of building the network in separate layers?
choices: Each layer can be changed or improved without redesigning the others
It removes the need for any wires
It makes every message exactly the same size
It lets one computer pretend to be many computers
items.explanation: Because a layer only promises a simple service to its neighbors, you can swap out or upgrade one layer (e.g., invent faster Wi-Fi) without touching the rest of the stack. That independence is the whole point.

items.prompt: The lesson compares network layers to floors in a building. What does each 'floor' (layer) talk to?
choices: Every other floor at the same time
Only the floors directly above and below it
Only the ground floor
No other floor — each works completely alone
items.explanation: A layer hands work to the layer just above and just below it, like passing things between adjacent floors. It does not reach across to distant layers, which keeps each layer's job simple.

items.prompt: Which of these is one of the 'jobs' the lesson says networking must handle?
choices: Charging the computer's battery
Finding the other machine and picking a path to it
Painting the screen a brighter color
Deciding which website is most popular
items.explanation: Locating the destination and choosing a path to reach it is a core part of the networking job. Battery, screen color, and popularity are unrelated to delivering a message.

items.prompt: Why does networking need a way to 'catch and fix errors'?
choices: Because users often type the wrong web address
Because real wires and Wi-Fi are imperfect, so some data gets garbled or lost
Because computers forget messages after a few seconds
Because every message must be translated into another language
items.explanation: Physical links are imperfect: signals fade, collide, and flip bits, so data can arrive corrupted or not at all. A layer's job is to detect that and recover, so the message still gets through.

items.prompt: An 'assembly line' is used as an analogy for layers because:
choices: Each station does one task and passes the work to the next station
Every station does the entire job by itself
The line only works if all stations are identical
Assembly lines never make mistakes
items.explanation: Like an assembly line, each layer performs one focused task and hands the result onward. That division of labor is exactly how layering tames a big problem.

items.prompt: A team invents a brand-new, faster kind of Wi-Fi. Thanks to layering, what can they avoid?
choices: They must rewrite every website on the internet
They can replace just the lowest 'send over the air' layer without redesigning the whole internet
They must get permission from every other computer first
They have to slow down all the other layers to match
items.explanation: Because each layer only promises a simple service to its neighbor, the new Wi-Fi swaps in at the bottom layer and everything above keeps working unchanged. That is the practical reward of separating concerns.

items.prompt: Which statement best matches the lesson's view of why the bytes arriving at a computer aren't automatically useful?
choices: Bytes are always useful the moment they arrive
The receiving program still has to make sense of the raw bytes
Bytes must be printed on paper before use
Only the fastest computer can read bytes
items.explanation: Raw bytes are just signals until some layer/program interprets them as a web page, message, or image. 'Making sense of the bytes' is one of the jobs layering assigns to an upper layer.

lessons.title: The Two Models: TCP/IP (4 Layers) and OSI (7 Layers)
lessons.content:
In the last lesson we said the network is built as a stack of layers, each doing one job. Now let's give those layers names. A **model** here just means an agreed-upon list of the layers and what each one does — a shared map so everyone describes the network the same way.

There are two famous maps. The first is the one the real internet actually uses: the **TCP/IP model**, with **4 layers**. From the bottom up:

- **Link** — send bits over one physical hop (a cable, a Wi-Fi connection).
- **Internet** — find machines across many hops and pick a path. This is where addresses live.
- **Transport** — deliver to the right program on the machine and, if asked, fix errors and keep data in order.
- **Application** — the actual program a person uses, like a web browser or email.

The second map is a teaching tool called the **OSI model** (Open Systems Interconnection), with **7 layers**, bottom up: **Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application**. A common memory phrase is "Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away." OSI splits the same work into more, finer slices so it is easier to discuss in detail.

The two maps line up like this: OSI's **Physical + Data Link** together do the job of TCP/IP's **Link**; OSI's **Network** matches **Internet**; **Transport** matches **Transport**; and OSI's top three (**Session, Presentation, Application**) all fold into TCP/IP's single **Application** layer.

**Recap:** TCP/IP's 4 layers (Link, Internet, Transport, Application) are what really runs; OSI's 7 layers are a finer teaching map. Learn both names and how they map.

items.prompt: In this context, what does a 'model' of the network mean?
choices: A physical plastic toy of a router
An agreed list of the layers and what each one does
The single fastest computer in a network
A law that bans certain websites
items.explanation: A model is just a shared map of the layers and their jobs, so everyone can describe and discuss the network the same way. It is not hardware or a rule.

items.prompt: How many layers does the TCP/IP model have, and is it the one the real internet uses?
choices: 7 layers, and it is only a teaching tool
4 layers, and yes it is what the real internet uses
5 layers, and it is used only for Wi-Fi
4 layers, but it is purely theoretical and unused
items.explanation: TCP/IP has 4 layers (Link, Internet, Transport, Application) and is the model the actual internet protocols were built around. OSI is the 7-layer teaching map.

items.prompt: Put the four TCP/IP layers in order from the BOTTOM (closest to the wire) to the TOP (closest to the user).
choices: Application, Transport, Internet, Link
Link, Internet, Transport, Application
Internet, Link, Application, Transport
Transport, Application, Link, Internet
items.explanation: Bottom to top, TCP/IP is Link (one physical hop), Internet (find machines across hops), Transport (reach the right program, fix errors), Application (the program the user sees).

items.prompt: Which TCP/IP layer's job is to send bits over a single physical hop, such as one cable or one Wi-Fi link?
choices: Application
Transport
Internet
Link
items.explanation: The Link layer handles getting bits across one direct connection. Internet handles crossing many hops, Transport handles reaching the right program, and Application is the user's program.

items.prompt: Which TCP/IP layer is responsible for finding machines across many hops and picking a path between them?
choices: Internet
Link
Application
Transport
items.explanation: The Internet layer locates machines across the wider network and chooses a path; this is where addresses live. The Link layer only covers one hop.

items.prompt: How many layers does the OSI model have, and what is its main purpose compared to TCP/IP?
choices: 4 layers; it is the real running model
7 layers; it is a finer teaching map that splits the work into more slices
3 layers; it replaces TCP/IP entirely
7 layers; it is only used inside Wi-Fi chips
items.explanation: OSI has 7 layers and exists mainly as a detailed teaching/discussion map. It slices the same work more finely than the 4-layer TCP/IP model the internet actually runs.

items.prompt: List the OSI 7 layers from the bottom up.
choices: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical
Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Link, Internet, Transport, Session, Network, Physical, Application
Physical, Network, Data Link, Transport, Application, Session, Presentation
items.explanation: Bottom up, OSI is Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application — the memory phrase 'Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away.' Choice A is the same list but top-down.

items.prompt: In the mapping between the two models, which OSI layers combine to do the job of TCP/IP's single Link layer?
choices: Session and Presentation
Physical and Data Link
Network and Transport
Transport and Application
items.explanation: TCP/IP's Link layer rolls OSI's two lowest layers — Physical and Data Link — into one. OSI's Network maps to Internet, and Transport maps to Transport.

items.prompt: OSI's top three layers (Session, Presentation, Application) correspond to which single TCP/IP layer?
choices: The Link layer
The Internet layer
The Transport layer
The Application layer
items.explanation: TCP/IP folds OSI's Session, Presentation, and Application into one Application layer, because in real systems that work is done inside the program and its libraries rather than as separate network layers.

items.prompt: A friend says 'just memorize OSI's 7 layers and ignore TCP/IP.' What is the best correction?
choices: Correct — TCP/IP is fake and never used
Learn both: TCP/IP's 4 layers are what actually runs, while OSI's 7 are a finer map for discussing details
Correct — OSI is what the internet runs on
Ignore both; layers are not a real idea
items.explanation: Both maps are useful. TCP/IP describes what genuinely runs on the wire; OSI gives a more detailed vocabulary for talking about the same work. Knowing how they line up is the goal.

lessons.title: Encapsulation: Wrapping the Message in Envelopes
lessons.content:
We now have names for the layers. But how does a layer actually 'do its job' on a real message? The answer is **encapsulation**, which is a long word for a simple idea: each layer wraps the data in its own little envelope before passing it down.

Picture mailing a letter. You write the **letter** (the message). You slip it into an **envelope** and write an address on the outside. The post office drops your envelope into a **mailbag**, and the mailbag goes on a **truck** with its own route. At every step the message gets wrapped in one more layer, and each handler reads only its own label. The truck driver never opens your envelope; the mail sorter never reads your letter.

Networks do exactly this. The extra information a layer writes on its 'envelope' is called a **header** — a small block of data added to the front. As your message goes **down** the stack, each layer adds its header:

- The Transport layer adds its header. The result is called a **segment**.
- The Internet layer wraps that and adds its header. Now it is a **packet**.
- The Link layer wraps that and adds its header. Now it is a **frame**, ready for the wire.

These names — **frame, packet, segment**, and the plain **data** at the top — are the official terms for 'the chunk at this layer.' Naming the chunk instantly tells you which layer someone means. A key rule: a lower layer never peeks inside the envelope it is carrying; to the Link layer, the whole packet inside is just cargo.

**Recap:** Encapsulation means each layer wraps the message in its own header on the way down (data → segment → packet → frame) and unwraps it on the way up.

items.prompt: What does 'encapsulation' mean in networking?
choices: Deleting parts of the message to make it smaller
Each layer wrapping the data in its own envelope (header) before passing it on
Encrypting the message so no one can read it
Sending the same message many times for safety
items.explanation: Encapsulation is the wrapping process: every layer adds its own header around the data it receives, like envelopes inside envelopes. It is not deletion, encryption, or repetition.

items.prompt: In the mailing-a-letter analogy, what plays the role of a layer's 'header'?
choices: The words written inside the letter itself
The address/label written on the outside of each wrapping
The weight of the envelope
The color of the mailbag
items.explanation: A header is the bit of addressing/control info a layer writes on the outside of its envelope — like the address on the envelope or the route on the mailbag. The letter's contents are the payload, not the header.

items.prompt: What exactly is a 'header'?
choices: A small block of control information a layer adds to the front of the data
The title of a web page
The first computer in the network
A type of error that corrupts messages
items.explanation: A header is a compact block of information a layer attaches (usually at the front) so the matching layer on the other side knows what to do. It is not a web-page title or a machine.

items.prompt: As a message travels DOWN the stack, what happens at each layer?
choices: Each layer removes a header
Each layer adds its own header, wrapping what it received
Each layer translates the message into a new language
Nothing changes until it reaches the wire
items.explanation: Going down, layers add headers (wrapping). Removing headers happens on the way up at the receiver, which is called decapsulation.

items.prompt: Match the chunk name to its layer: at the Internet layer, the chunk of data is called a...
choices: Frame
Packet
Segment
Letter
items.explanation: The Internet layer's chunk is a packet. A frame is the Link layer's chunk, and a segment is the Transport layer's chunk.

items.prompt: What is the chunk of data called once the Transport layer has added its header?
choices: A frame
A packet
A segment
A header
items.explanation: After the Transport layer wraps the data, the chunk is a segment. It becomes a packet at the Internet layer and a frame at the Link layer.

items.prompt: Put the chunk names in the order they are formed as data goes DOWN the stack, starting from the top (Application).
choices: Frame → Packet → Segment → Data
Data → Segment → Packet → Frame
Segment → Data → Frame → Packet
Packet → Frame → Data → Segment
items.explanation: Top to bottom: it starts as application data, the Transport layer makes a segment, the Internet layer makes a packet, and the Link layer makes a frame for the wire.

items.prompt: To the Link layer carrying a frame, the packet wrapped inside it is treated as:
choices: Something it must read and rewrite field by field
Just cargo (payload) it carries without looking inside
An error that must be deleted
Its own header
items.explanation: A layer reads only its own header and treats whatever is inside as opaque cargo. This 'don't peek inside' rule is what keeps the layers independent.

items.prompt: When the message reaches the receiving computer and travels UP its stack, what does each layer do with the header its partner added?
choices: Adds another copy of it
Strips off (removes) its own header and hands the rest up
Sends it back to the sender
Encrypts it
items.explanation: On the way up, each layer removes the matching header its counterpart added and passes the inner data upward. This unwrapping is decapsulation — the mirror image of encapsulation.

items.prompt: Why is the 'each handler reads only its own label' rule (the truck driver never opens the envelope) so useful?
choices: It makes messages travel faster than light
It keeps layers independent, so each can do its job without understanding the others
It means only one layer is ever needed
It guarantees no message is ever lost
items.explanation: Because each layer only handles its own header, layers stay independent and simple — the same separation-of-concerns benefit from lesson 1. It does not change physics or guarantee perfect delivery.

lessons.title: One Message: Down Your Stack and Up the Other
lessons.content:
Let's tie it all together by following one real action: you click a link and your computer asks a faraway computer (a **server**) for a web page. A **protocol** — an agreed set of rules for how computers talk — governs each step.

The request starts at the **Application** layer (your browser) as plain **data**. It moves **down** your computer's stack. The **Transport** layer wraps it into a **segment**, stamping a **port** number — think of it as picking which program on the far machine should get this (web pages, email, and games each use different ports). The **Internet** layer wraps that into a **packet**, stamping the **address** of the server — which machine to find among billions. The **Link** layer wraps that into a **frame** and finally turns it into signals on the wire or Wi-Fi.

Those signals travel across the network — often hopping through several machines in between. When they reach the server, the message climbs **up** its stack. The Link layer unwraps the frame, the Internet layer unwraps the packet, the Transport layer unwraps the segment, and the Application layer (the web server program) finally reads your request and sends a page back — which makes the whole trip in reverse. Each layer 'talks to' the matching layer on the other side, but the bits really go all the way down one side and all the way up the other.

Who decides what every header looks like so machines from different makers agree? **Standards bodies.** The main one for the internet is the **IETF** (Internet Engineering Task Force), which publishes its rules as numbered documents called **RFCs** (Requests for Comments). An RFC is just the public write-up of a protocol.

**Recap:** A request goes down your stack gaining headers, crosses the network, and climbs the server's stack shedding them; shared rules (protocols), agreed in IETF RFCs, let any two machines understand each other.

items.prompt: What is a 'protocol'?
choices: A single very fast cable
An agreed set of rules for how computers talk to each other
The name of the biggest computer on the internet
A program that only displays pictures
items.explanation: A protocol is an agreed set of rules so two computers can understand each other — what to send, in what order, and what each part means. It is not hardware or a single program.

items.prompt: When you request a web page, at which layer does the message START on your computer?
choices: The Link layer
The Internet layer
The Application layer (your browser)
The Transport layer
items.explanation: The request begins at the top, in the Application layer (the browser), then travels down through Transport, Internet, and Link to reach the wire.

items.prompt: As your request leaves your computer, in what direction does it move through the layers?
choices: Up the stack, from Link to Application
Down the stack, from Application to Link
It skips straight to the Internet layer
It stays in one layer the whole time
items.explanation: On the sending side the message moves DOWN: Application → Transport → Internet → Link, gaining a header at each step before hitting the wire.

items.prompt: An address (stamped by the Internet layer) and a port (stamped by the Transport layer) do which jobs?
choices: Address picks the program; port picks the machine
Address picks the machine; port picks which program on it
Both pick the machine
Both pick the color of the data
items.explanation: The address says which machine to find among billions; the port says which program on that machine should receive the data. (You will study addresses and ports in detail in later lessons.)

items.prompt: When the message arrives at the server, what direction does it travel through the server's layers?
choices: Down, from Application to Link
Up, from Link to Application
It bounces back without entering the server
Sideways between two servers
items.explanation: At the receiver the message climbs UP the stack: the Link layer unwraps the frame, then Internet, then Transport, and finally the Application layer reads it — the mirror of the sender's downward trip.

items.prompt: The lesson says each layer 'talks to' the matching layer on the other computer. What is literally happening to the bits?
choices: The bits jump directly between the two Transport layers
The bits travel all the way down one stack, across the network, and all the way up the other stack
The bits stay only in the Application layer
Each layer sends its own separate copy over a separate wire
items.explanation: Same-layer 'conversation' is a useful way to think, but physically the bits go down the sender's whole stack, across the wire (often via in-between machines), and up the receiver's whole stack.

items.prompt: What is a 'server' in this story?
choices: A waiter in a restaurant
A computer that receives your request and sends back the web page
The wire between two computers
A header added by the Link layer
items.explanation: Here a server is simply the faraway computer that receives your request and responds with the page. It is an ordinary participant in the same layered message exchange.

items.prompt: Why do we need standards bodies like the IETF?
choices: To sell internet cables
So machines from different makers agree on what every header and rule means
To make the internet slower on purpose
To decide which websites are allowed to exist
items.explanation: Standards bodies publish agreed rules so independently built machines interoperate — everyone formats and reads headers the same way. They do not sell hardware or police content.

items.prompt: What is an RFC?
choices: A type of cable connector
A numbered public document that writes up an internet protocol's rules
A program that blocks viruses
The fastest available Wi-Fi speed
items.explanation: RFC stands for Request for Comments — the numbered documents the IETF uses to publish protocol rules. It is the public specification, not hardware or software.

items.prompt: Pulling the whole sub-topic together, which summary is correct?
choices: Layers are pointless; one program does everything
A message gains headers going down the sender's stack, crosses the network, and sheds them going up the receiver's stack, all guided by shared protocols
Messages only ever stay on one computer
Only the Link layer ever touches the data
items.explanation: That is the core picture: encapsulation on the way down, decapsulation on the way up, with each layer doing one job under agreed protocols — exactly why we split networking into layers in the first place.

lessons.title: The Bottom Layer: Turning Bits Into a Signal
lessons.content:
You already know a network splits its work into **layers** (stacked jobs, each talking only to its neighbors). The **physical layer** is the bottom one. It has just one job, and it is wonderfully simple to picture.

Everything a computer stores is **bits** — 1s and 0s. But a 1 is just an idea; you cannot mail an idea down a wire. So the physical layer's job is to turn those bits into something that can physically *travel*, send it across the gap, and turn it back into the same bits at the other end. The 'something that travels' is called a **signal** — a physical thing the far end can detect and measure.

Think of **Morse code**: you flip a light or a buzzer on and off, and the pattern of on/off carries letters. That on/off is a signal. The physical layer does the same trick, just far faster, using one of three things: **electrical voltage** (a higher or lower 'push' of electricity) on a copper wire, **flashes of light** sent down a thin glass thread, or **radio waves** sent through the open air. At the far end, a receiver watches that signal and writes down the bits again.

The three common ways to carry a signal are called **media** (one is a *medium*): copper cable, fiber-optic, and wireless. We will meet each one next.

**Recap:** The physical layer is the bottom layer. Its only job is bits in -> a physical signal that travels (voltage, light, or radio) -> the same bits out the other side.

items.prompt: What is the one job of the physical layer?
choices: To decide which website you are visiting
To turn bits into a physical signal that can travel, then turn it back into the same bits at the far end
To store your files safely on a hard drive
To pick the fastest path across the whole internet
items.explanation: The physical layer is the bottom of the stack and does exactly one thing: it converts 1s and 0s into something physical (voltage, light, or radio waves), sends it, and converts it back to bits. Choosing websites and picking routes are jobs for higher layers; storing files is not a networking job at all.

items.prompt: In networking, what is a 'signal'?
choices: A warning message that something has gone wrong
A physical thing the far end can detect and measure, such as a voltage, a flash of light, or a radio wave
Another word for a single bit
The name of the cable that connects two computers
items.explanation: A signal is the physical thing that actually travels and carries the bits: a voltage on copper, light in fiber, or a radio wave in the air. It is not the bit itself (a bit is the idea being carried), and it is not the cable (the cable is the medium the signal travels through).

items.prompt: Why can't a computer just send the bit '1' down a wire directly?
choices: A wire can only carry the bit '0', never a '1'
A bit is just stored information; to travel it must first be turned into something physical like a voltage or a pulse of light
Wires are too slow to carry a '1'
The '1' would arrive as a '0' unless it is encrypted first
items.explanation: A bit is an abstract value, not a physical object, so it cannot move on its own. The physical layer represents it with something measurable (high vs low voltage, light on vs off, etc.). Wires carry both 1s and 0s fine, and speed or encryption is not the reason a bit needs to be turned into a signal.

items.prompt: Morse code (flipping a light on and off to spell letters) is used in this lesson as an everyday example of what idea?
choices: Turning a pattern of on/off into meaning a far-away person can read
How computers store files on disk
Why fiber is faster than copper
How the internet finds the shortest route
items.explanation: Morse code shows the core physical-layer idea: an on/off signal carries information that someone at the other end can decode. That is exactly what the physical layer does, only much faster and automatically. The other options are about different topics entirely.

items.prompt: Which three things does the physical layer commonly use to carry a signal?
choices: Passwords, files, and folders
Electrical voltage on copper, flashes of light in fiber, and radio waves in the air
Web pages, emails, and videos
Routers, switches, and firewalls
items.explanation: The three common ways to physically carry bits are voltage on copper wire, light in glass fiber, and radio waves through the air. Web pages and emails are content carried by higher layers; routers and firewalls are devices that operate above the physical layer.

items.prompt: The word 'medium' (plural 'media') in this lesson means what?
choices: A medium-sized network, between small and large
The physical path a signal travels through, such as copper cable, fiber, or air
A program that plays music and video
The middle layer of the network stack
items.explanation: A medium is simply the physical stuff a signal moves through: a copper cable, a glass fiber, or the open air for radio. It has nothing to do with size, media players, or being in the middle of the stack.

items.prompt: When the physical layer is working correctly, what should come out of the receiving end?
choices: A web page, fully drawn on the screen
The same bits that went in at the sending end
A signal that is stronger than the one that was sent
A list of every computer on the network
items.explanation: The physical layer's promise is faithful delivery of bits: the same 1s and 0s come out as went in. Drawing a web page is a much-higher-layer job, the received signal is normally weaker (not stronger), and listing computers is not a physical-layer task.

items.prompt: Where does the physical layer sit in the stack of layers?
choices: At the very top, closest to the user's app
At the very bottom, closest to the actual wire, fiber, or air
It floats between all the other layers at once
It sits outside the stack and is not really a layer
items.explanation: The physical layer is the bottom layer, right next to the real-world medium. Everything else rides on top of it. The top layers are the ones closest to the user's application; the physical layer is as far from the user and as close to the hardware as you can get.

lessons.title: Three Media, Speed, and Putting Bits on a Wave
lessons.content:
We send signals over three **media**: copper, fiber, and wireless. None is best at everything, so the choice is always a trade-off between **cost**, **distance** (how far before the signal gets too weak), **speed**, and **interference** (unwanted noise getting in).

- **Copper cable** carries electrical voltage. It is cheap and familiar (it is the cable plugged into most desk computers), but the signal fades over short distances and copper acts like an antenna, so it picks up electrical noise.
- **Fiber-optic** sends flashes of light down a hair-thin glass thread, like light through a glass pipe. It costs more and is more delicate, but it carries data the fastest, travels the farthest, and because it uses light it is immune to electrical interference.
- **Wireless / radio** sends radio waves through the open air. There is no cable to run, which is convenient, but everyone shares the same air, so it suffers the most interference and is usually slower and shorter-range than fiber.

Two words people mix up. **Bandwidth** and **bit rate** both relate to 'how fast,' but: **bit rate** is how many bits you actually push per second (measured in bits per second, *bps*) - the headline 'speed.' **Bandwidth** is the *capacity* of the medium - how much room it has to carry a signal. More bandwidth means room for a higher bit rate, the way a wider pipe can carry more water.

How do bits actually ride on a signal? Two plain ideas. **Line coding** is the rulebook for how a 1 or a 0 maps to a voltage level on a wire (for example, high voltage = 1, low voltage = 0). **Modulation** means taking a steady wave (a *carrier*) and *changing* it - making it bigger or smaller, faster or slower - so those changes spell out the bits. Either way, the receiver watches the signal and reads the bits back.

**Recap:** Copper, fiber, and wireless each trade off cost, distance, speed, and interference. Bit rate is bits-per-second; bandwidth is the capacity that allows it. Line coding maps bits to voltage levels; modulation changes a wave to carry bits.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the copper vs fiber vs wireless trade-off?
choices: Fiber is best in every way, so the other two are never used
Each medium trades off cost, distance, speed, and interference, so the best choice depends on the situation
Wireless is always the fastest because it has no cable to slow it down
Copper is immune to interference, which is why it is used everywhere
items.explanation: No single medium wins on every measure, so engineers weigh cost, distance, speed, and interference for each job. Fiber is fastest and longest-reach but pricier; wireless is convenient but the most interference-prone and usually slower; copper is cheap but short-range and noise-prone, not interference-immune.

items.prompt: Why is fiber-optic immune to electrical interference, while copper is not?
choices: Fiber carries bits as flashes of light, which electrical noise cannot disturb, while copper carries electrical voltage that nearby electrical noise can add to
Fiber is wrapped in metal shielding and copper never is
Fiber moves so fast that noise cannot keep up with it
Copper is older, and old cables always attract more noise
items.explanation: Fiber uses light, and stray electrical fields simply do not affect a beam of light, so it shrugs off electrical interference. Copper carries an electrical signal, and nearby electrical noise adds onto that signal. It is the light-vs-electricity difference, not shielding, speed, or age.

items.prompt: What is the main everyday advantage of wireless over copper and fiber?
choices: It is always faster than both
There is no cable to run, so devices can move around freely
It never suffers from interference
It can carry signals an unlimited distance
items.explanation: Wireless's big win is convenience: no physical cable, so phones and laptops can roam. It is usually slower than fiber, suffers the most interference because the air is shared, and has limited range. The freedom from cables is the real trade-off it buys.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is 'bit rate'?
choices: How many bits you actually send per second
How heavy the cable is
The number of computers on the network
How long a cable is allowed to be
items.explanation: Bit rate is the headline speed: bits pushed per second (bits per second, bps). It is not about cable weight, length, or how many machines are connected. A higher bit rate means more data moved each second.

items.prompt: The lesson compares bandwidth to a water pipe. What does 'more bandwidth' give you?
choices: A heavier cable that lasts longer
More room in the medium to carry a signal, allowing a higher bit rate
A guarantee that no bits will ever be lost
A shorter distance the signal can travel
items.explanation: Bandwidth is capacity, like the width of a pipe: a wider pipe can carry more water, and more bandwidth makes room for a higher bit rate. It does not by itself guarantee error-free delivery, change the cable's durability, or shorten the reach.

items.prompt: What does 'line coding' mean?
choices: Writing software in straight lines
The rulebook for how a 1 or a 0 maps to a voltage level on a wire
Splitting one cable into several smaller cables
Encrypting data so attackers cannot read it
items.explanation: Line coding is simply the agreed rule for representing bits as voltage levels, for example high voltage for a 1 and low voltage for a 0, so the receiver can read them back. It is not programming, cable-splitting, or encryption.

items.prompt: In plain language, what does 'modulation' mean?
choices: Deleting bits to make a message shorter
Taking a steady wave and changing it (bigger/smaller, faster/slower) so the changes spell out the bits
Turning a wireless signal into a wired one
Choosing which website to load
items.explanation: Modulation means changing a carrier wave so its variations carry data: make the wave taller for a 1 and shorter for a 0, for instance. It does not delete data, convert wireless to wired, or pick websites; those are unrelated.

items.prompt: Look at the modulation diagram: a big wave means 1 and a small wave means 0. How does the receiver recover the bits?
choices: It counts how many cables are plugged in
It measures the size of the wave in each time slot and writes down 1 for a big wave, 0 for a small one
It asks the sender to repeat every bit out loud
It guesses randomly and corrects later
items.explanation: With this kind of modulation the information is in the wave's size, so the receiver simply measures each slot: a big swing is a 1, a small swing is a 0. That is the whole idea of reading a modulated signal; no counting cables, repeating, or guessing is involved.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation best fits choosing copper over fiber?
choices: Linking two cities hundreds of kilometers apart at the highest possible speed
Connecting a desktop computer to a wall jack a few meters away, cheaply
Sending data where absolutely no electrical interference can be tolerated
A link that must run for tens of kilometers without any signal boost
items.explanation: Copper shines for short, cheap, everyday runs like a desktop to a nearby jack. Long city-to-city links, interference-free needs, and very long unboosted runs all point to fiber, which is faster, longer-reach, and immune to electrical noise.

lessons.title: Noise, Limits, and the Cables That Connect It All
lessons.content:
A signal does not travel forever unharmed. Two things wear it down. **Attenuation** means the signal gets *weaker* the farther it goes - like a voice fading as you walk away from someone. **Noise** (or **interference**) means unwanted junk gets mixed in - like trying to be heard across a loud, crowded room. Picture shouting across that room: the farther away your friend is, the fainter your voice (attenuation), and the more other chatter drowns you out (noise). Eventually the listener can no longer tell your words apart, and the receiver makes mistakes - it reads a 1 as a 0. That is why long copper runs and weak Wi-Fi get unreliable.

This leads to a key idea: every channel has a **maximum data rate**. You cannot send bits infinitely fast. The limit goes up with **more bandwidth** (more room to work in) and a **cleaner signal** (less noise compared to signal). In short: *more bandwidth plus a cleaner signal equals more bits per second*. That is the whole idea behind the famous Shannon and Nyquist results - there is a ceiling, set by how wide and how clean your channel is. (We will skip the formulas; the idea is what matters.)

Finally, the cables and connectors are standardized so gear from different makers fits together. Copper **Ethernet cable** comes in graded **categories** - you may have seen 'Cat5e,' 'Cat6,' or 'Cat8' on a cable. Higher numbers support more bandwidth and faster speeds. **Fiber** comes in two flavors: **multimode**, with a wider core, cheaper and used for shorter distances; and **single-mode**, with a very thin core that carries light the farthest, used for long hauls.

One more pair of terms about direction. A link is **half duplex** if data can flow both ways but only one direction at a time (like a walkie-talkie: you talk, then I talk). It is **full duplex** if both directions can happen at once (like a phone call where both people can speak together). Modern wired links are full duplex.

**Recap:** Attenuation weakens a signal over distance and noise corrupts it, so every channel has a max data rate - more bandwidth and a cleaner signal allow more bits. Cable categories (Cat5e/6/8) and fiber types (multimode/single-mode) are standardized, and links are half duplex (one way at a time) or full duplex (both at once).

items.prompt: What does 'attenuation' mean?
choices: A signal getting weaker the farther it travels
A signal speeding up over distance
Two cables being plugged into the same port
Bits being encrypted for safety
items.explanation: Attenuation is the natural weakening of a signal as it travels, just as a voice fades with distance. It is not about speeding up, plugging in cables, or encryption. Attenuation is a big reason links have distance limits.

items.prompt: The lesson uses 'shouting across a noisy, crowded room' to explain which idea?
choices: Why fiber costs more than copper
Signal versus noise: your fading voice is the signal, the crowd's chatter is the noise that can drown it out
How a switch learns where computers are
Why bandwidth is measured in bits per second
items.explanation: The crowded room captures signal versus noise: your voice (the signal) fades with distance (attenuation) while other chatter (noise) competes with it. When noise overwhelms the signal, the listener mishears, just as a receiver misreads bits. The other options are unrelated topics.

items.prompt: Why do signals become unreliable over long distances or with lots of interference?
choices: The bits get heavier and sink in the cable
Attenuation makes the signal faint and noise mixes in, so the receiver can no longer tell a 1 from a 0
The internet automatically slows down at night
Cables run out of bits to send
items.explanation: As distance grows the signal weakens (attenuation) and unwanted noise piles on, until the receiver cannot reliably distinguish 1s from 0s and makes errors. Bits have no weight, cables do not 'run out' of them, and time of day is not the cause.

items.prompt: As a concept (no formulas), what raises a channel's maximum data rate?
choices: More bandwidth and a cleaner signal (less noise) both let you send more bits per second
Only the brand of the cable matters
A longer cable always allows more bits per second
Lowering the bandwidth so noise has less room
items.explanation: The capacity idea behind Shannon and Nyquist is that more bandwidth (more room) and a cleaner signal (higher signal compared to noise) together raise the ceiling on bits per second. A longer cable actually hurts (more attenuation), brand is not the principle, and shrinking bandwidth lowers, not raises, the limit.

items.prompt: The Shannon/Nyquist result is best summarized for a beginner as which idea?
choices: Signals can travel infinitely fast if the cable is new
There is a ceiling on how fast a channel can carry bits, set by how wide (bandwidth) and how clean (low noise) it is
Noise always helps a signal get through
Only wireless links have a speed limit
items.explanation: The one-line takeaway is that every channel has a maximum data rate determined by its bandwidth and its noise level. Speeds are never infinite, noise always hurts, and the limit applies to all media, wired and wireless alike.

items.prompt: On an Ethernet cable you see 'Cat6' instead of 'Cat5e'. What does the higher category generally mean?
choices: It is a thinner, cheaper cable
It supports more bandwidth and faster speeds
It can only be used outdoors
It works without being plugged in
items.explanation: Cable categories are graded: a higher number like Cat6 (versus Cat5e) supports more bandwidth and therefore higher speeds. Higher categories are typically built better, not thinner-and-cheaper, are not outdoor-only, and still need to be plugged in.

items.prompt: What is the main difference between single-mode and multimode fiber?
choices: Single-mode carries electricity; multimode carries light
Single-mode has a very thin core and reaches the farthest; multimode has a wider core, is cheaper, and is used for shorter distances
Multimode is always faster and longer-range than single-mode
Single-mode is wireless and multimode is wired
items.explanation: Both are fiber and both carry light. Single-mode's very thin core sends light the farthest, ideal for long hauls; multimode's wider core is cheaper and suits shorter runs. Multimode is not the longer-range option, and neither is wireless.

items.prompt: A walkie-talkie lets you talk OR listen, but not both at the same time. Which term describes this?
choices: Full duplex
Half duplex
Attenuation
Bandwidth
items.explanation: Half duplex means data can flow both ways but only one direction at a time, exactly like a walkie-talkie. Full duplex would allow both directions at once (like a phone call). Attenuation and bandwidth describe signal strength and capacity, not direction.

items.prompt: A phone call where both people can speak and be heard at the same time is an example of what?
choices: Half duplex
Full duplex
No duplex (one-way only)
Interference
items.explanation: Full duplex means both directions can happen simultaneously, like a normal phone call. Half duplex would force one speaker at a time; one-way only would block a reply entirely. Interference is unwanted noise, not a description of direction.

subtopics.title: Data Link: Ethernet, Framing, MAC & Switching

lessons.title: The Data Link Layer: Frames and MAC Addresses
lessons.content:
## Getting data to the device next door

You already know the physical layer just pushes raw bits (1s and 0s) down a wire or through the air. But a stream of bare bits is not very useful on its own. Which bits belong together? Who is this data for? Did any bits get garbled along the way?

The **data link layer** answers those questions. It sits **just above** the physical layer, and its job is simple to state: **move data reliably between two devices on the same local network** — for example, your laptop and the Wi-Fi router sitting in your house. "Local network" just means the devices near you that are directly connected, like everything in your home or one office.

## Frames and addresses

The data link layer takes the bits and groups them into **frames**. A **frame** is a chunk of data with a clear *start*, some *address labels*, the actual *payload* (the data being carried), and an *error-check* value at the end. Think of it like an envelope: it has a front (who it's for, who it's from), the letter inside, and a tamper-check seal.

To write the address on that envelope, every network device uses a **MAC address** (Media Access Control address). A MAC address is a **permanent hardware ID burned into the network chip at the factory**. It is **48 bits** long and is written as six pairs of hex digits, like `a1:b2:c3:44:55:66`. Because it is built into the hardware, it normally never changes, and it identifies exactly **one device on the local network**.

So a frame says, in effect: "From MAC `aa:aa:…`, to MAC `bb:bb:…`, here is the data." The address makes sure the frame reaches the one nearby device it is meant for, the same way an apartment number gets a letter to the right resident in your building.

## MAC vs IP — a quick heads-up

You may have heard of **IP addresses** too. Here is the key difference, which the next sub-topics will build on: a **MAC address is the local, permanent hardware address** used to reach a device on the *same* network, while an **IP address is a logical address** used to find devices *across* different networks. For now, just remember: MAC = local and permanent; IP = logical and used to travel between networks.

## Recap

The data link layer turns raw bits into addressed **frames** and delivers them between two devices on the **same local network**, using permanent **MAC addresses** to label who each frame is for.

items.prompt: Study the diagram comparing a MAC address and an IP address. Based on it, which statement is correct?
choices: A MAC address changes every time you move your device to a new network
A MAC address is a permanent, local hardware ID, while an IP address is a logical address used to cross networks
An IP address is burned into the chip and a MAC address is assigned by software
MAC and IP addresses are two names for the exact same thing
items.explanation: As the diagram shows, the MAC address is the permanent, factory-burned hardware ID that identifies a device on the local network, while the IP address is a logical (software-assigned) address used to reach devices across different networks. They do different jobs at different layers.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is the main job of the data link layer?
choices: To choose the best path for data across the whole Internet
To move data reliably between two devices on the same local network
To convert text into the radio waves used by Wi-Fi
To decide which website a browser should open
items.explanation: The data link layer sits just above the physical layer and handles delivery between two directly connected devices on the same local network (such as your laptop and your home router). Choosing paths across many networks is the job of a higher layer (the network layer, using IP).

items.prompt: What is a 'frame' in networking?
choices: A single bit travelling down a wire
A chunk of data with a start, address labels, a payload, and an error-check at the end
The metal case that holds a network switch
A list of every website a device has visited
items.explanation: A frame is the data link layer's 'envelope': it groups bits into one unit with a clear start, address labels (who it is for and from), the payload (the data being carried), and an error-check value at the end. A single bit is just part of the physical layer's bit stream.

items.prompt: How long is a MAC address, and how is it usually written?
choices: 32 bits, written as four numbers separated by dots like 192.168.1.5
48 bits, written as six pairs of hex digits like a1:b2:c3:44:55:66
8 bits, written as a single number from 0 to 255
128 bits, written as eight groups separated by colons
items.explanation: A MAC address is 48 bits long and is written as six pairs of hexadecimal digits, for example a1:b2:c3:44:55:66. The dotted-decimal form like 192.168.1.5 is an IPv4 address, and the 128-bit colon form is IPv6 — both are different from a MAC address.

items.prompt: Where does a device's MAC address come from, and how often does it normally change?
choices: It is typed in by the user and changes every day
It is burned into the network chip at the factory and normally never changes
It is handed out fresh by the Wi-Fi router every time the device connects
It is calculated from the current time, so it is always different
items.explanation: A MAC address is a permanent hardware ID burned into the network chip when it is manufactured, so it normally stays the same for the life of the device. The address that a router hands out when you connect is the IP address, which is a different, logical address.

items.prompt: A MAC address vs an IP address — what is the key difference?
choices: A MAC address is the local, permanent hardware address; an IP address is a logical address used to cross between networks
A MAC address is used on the Internet; an IP address only works inside your house
A MAC address is longer than an IP address and is always written in decimal
There is no difference; both are assigned by the factory and never change
items.explanation: This is one of the most important distinctions in networking. The MAC address is the permanent, hardware-level address that identifies a device on the *local* network. The IP address is a logical, software-assigned address used to reach devices *across* different networks. Data crossing the Internet relies on IP; delivery to the device right next to you relies on MAC.

items.prompt: Why does a frame need to carry a destination MAC address at all?
choices: So the data link layer can deliver the frame to the correct nearby device instead of just every device
So the frame can be encrypted before it is sent
So the receiver knows which website the data came from
So the physical layer can decide what voltage to use
items.explanation: The destination MAC is the address label on the envelope: it tells the local network which specific nearby device the frame is meant for, just as an apartment number directs a letter to one resident. Without it, there would be no way to single out the intended device. Encryption and website identity are handled by higher layers.

items.prompt: A good real-world analogy for a MAC address is:
choices: The temporary ticket number you get at a deli counter
A resident's permanent ID that an envelope is addressed to so it reaches the right apartment
The speed limit posted on a highway
The colour of the envelope the letter is mailed in
items.explanation: A MAC address is like a resident's permanent ID used to address an envelope to one specific apartment in a building — it is fixed and identifies exactly one recipient on the local network. A deli ticket changes each visit, which is more like a temporary IP address than a permanent MAC.

items.prompt: Which layer does the data link layer sit directly above, and what does it receive from it?
choices: The application layer; it receives finished web pages
The physical layer; it receives a raw stream of bits
The transport layer; it receives reliable connections
There is no layer below it; the data link layer is the lowest
items.explanation: The data link layer sits just above the physical layer. The physical layer moves raw bits (1s and 0s) over the wire or air, and the data link layer organizes those bits into addressed frames. The physical layer is the lowest layer, not the data link layer.

items.prompt: Two laptops are connected to the same home Wi-Fi router. At the data link layer, how does one laptop's frame reach the other?
choices: It is addressed using the other laptop's permanent MAC address on the local network
It must travel across the Internet and come back
It is sent to every device in the entire city until the right one answers
It uses the website's domain name as the address
items.explanation: Both laptops are on the same local network, so the data link layer delivers the frame directly using the destination laptop's MAC address. There is no need to leave the local network. Domain names and crossing the Internet involve higher layers, not local frame delivery.

lessons.title: Inside an Ethernet Frame, Field by Field
lessons.content:
## What is Ethernet?

**Ethernet** is the dominant set of rules for **wired local networks**. When you plug a cable into a wall jack or into the back of a router, you are almost certainly using Ethernet. It defines exactly how a frame is laid out so that any Ethernet device can understand any other.

## The envelope, laid out left to right

Let's open up one Ethernet frame and read it from start to finish. Picture it as a row of labeled boxes:

1. **Preamble + Start delimiter** — a short "wake-up" pattern that lets the receiver's clock lock onto the incoming bits. It is *not* counted as part of the frame and is *not* error-checked. Think of it as the receiver clearing its throat before the real message.
2. **Destination MAC (6 bytes)** — who the frame is **for**.
3. **Source MAC (6 bytes)** — who the frame is **from**.
4. **Type (2 bytes)** — what kind of data is inside, so the receiver knows how to read it (for example, a value meaning "this carries an IP packet").
5. **Payload (46 to 1500 bytes)** — the actual data being carried.
6. **FCS (4 bytes)** — the **Frame Check Sequence**, the error-check at the very end.

## What the FCS is for

Wires and radios are not perfect; a bit can flip on the way. The **FCS is an error-check value** the sender calculates from the rest of the frame using a math formula called a **CRC** (Cyclic Redundancy Check — you don't need the math, just the idea). The receiver runs the same calculation. If its answer matches the FCS, the frame is almost certainly intact. **If it does not match, the frame is corrupt and is thrown away.**

Here is the key limit: Ethernet **detects** errors but does **not fix** them. A bad frame is simply discarded. Getting the data resent (if anyone bothers) is left to a higher layer. So the FCS is a smoke detector, not a fire department: it tells you something is wrong, but it does not repair the damage.

## Recap

An Ethernet frame is a labeled envelope: destination MAC, source MAC, a type field, the payload, and an FCS error-check at the end. The FCS lets the receiver detect a corrupted frame and discard it — Ethernet catches errors, it does not correct them.

items.prompt: Look at the labeled Ethernet frame diagram. Reading left to right, which order are the main fields in?
choices: FCS, payload, type, source MAC, destination MAC
Destination MAC, source MAC, type, payload, FCS
Payload, destination MAC, FCS, type, source MAC
Type, payload, destination MAC, source MAC, FCS
items.explanation: As the diagram lays out, after the wake-up preamble the frame goes: destination MAC (who it's for), source MAC (who it's from), type (what's inside), payload (the data), and finally the FCS error-check at the end.

items.prompt: What is Ethernet, in plain terms?
choices: The single cable that connects your house to the Internet provider
The dominant set of rules for wired local networks
A brand of Wi-Fi router
The program that opens web pages
items.explanation: Ethernet is the dominant standard (set of rules) for wired local networks — it defines how frames are formatted and sent over cables so any Ethernet device understands any other. It is not a single cable, a brand, or an application.

items.prompt: Within the frame, what do the destination MAC and source MAC fields tell you?
choices: The size of the payload and the speed of the link
Who the frame is for (destination) and who it is from (source)
Which website sent the data and which one will receive it
The error-check value and the wake-up pattern
items.explanation: The destination MAC says which device the frame is for, and the source MAC says which device it came from — the 'to' and 'from' on the envelope. Sizes, speeds, and the error-check are separate concerns, and websites are identified far higher up the stack.

items.prompt: What does the 'type' field in an Ethernet frame do?
choices: It tells the receiver what kind of data is inside, so it knows how to read the payload
It sets how many volts the wire should use
It counts how many devices are on the network
It stores the sender's permanent MAC address
items.explanation: The type field identifies what the payload contains (for example, a value meaning the payload is an IP packet), so the receiver hands the data to the right software. Voltages are a physical-layer concern, and the sender's address lives in the source MAC field.

items.prompt: What is the FCS (Frame Check Sequence) used for?
choices: To encrypt the payload so no one can read it
To let the receiver detect whether the frame was corrupted in transit
To store the destination MAC address
To speed up the connection
items.explanation: The FCS is an error-check value at the end of the frame. The sender computes it from the rest of the frame; the receiver recomputes it and compares. A mismatch means the frame was damaged. The FCS does not encrypt, address, or speed up anything — it only detects errors.

items.prompt: A frame arrives and the receiver's recomputed check does NOT match the FCS. What happens?
choices: Ethernet repairs the damaged bits using the FCS and keeps the frame
The frame is thrown away; Ethernet detects errors but does not fix them
The frame is sent back to the sender at the data link layer for correction
The receiver ignores the FCS and uses the frame anyway
items.explanation: A mismatch means the frame is corrupt, so Ethernet simply discards it. Ethernet's FCS only *detects* errors — it cannot correct them, and Ethernet does not automatically resend the frame itself. Any recovery is left to a higher layer.

items.prompt: Why are the preamble and start delimiter NOT counted as part of the frame or covered by the FCS?
choices: They are just a wake-up timing pattern that helps the receiver lock onto the bits, not real data
They are encrypted separately for security
They contain the most important data, so they get their own check
They are added by the user, not the hardware
items.explanation: The preamble and start delimiter are a fixed 'wake-up' pattern that lets the receiver's clock synchronize and find where the frame begins. They carry no message data, so they sit outside the frame proper and outside the FCS calculation.

items.prompt: A good analogy for the FCS is:
choices: A spell-checker that quietly fixes your typos for you
A smoke detector — it tells you something is wrong but does not put out the fire
A return address so the letter can be mailed back
A stamp that pays for delivery
items.explanation: The FCS is like a smoke detector: it alerts the receiver that the frame is damaged, but it does not repair anything. A spell-checker that auto-corrects would be error *correction*, which Ethernet's FCS does not do.

items.prompt: The payload of a standard Ethernet frame can hold roughly how much data?
choices: Exactly 1 byte, always
Up to about 1500 bytes
Up to about 1 million bytes
There is no limit at all
items.explanation: A standard Ethernet payload holds up to about 1500 bytes (with a small minimum so frames are not too short). This 1500-byte ceiling is the usual maximum amount of data per frame; bigger messages are split across many frames.

items.prompt: If a frame's data gets corrupted and dropped, whose responsibility is it to recover that lost data?
choices: Ethernet automatically resends every dropped frame itself
A higher layer must handle any resending; Ethernet just discards the bad frame
The preamble re-sends the frame
Nothing can ever recover it under any circumstances
items.explanation: Ethernet's job ends at detecting and discarding the corrupt frame. Whether the data is resent is decided by a higher layer (you will meet protocols that do this later). So Ethernet does not recover the data itself, but recovery is still possible above it.

lessons.title: Switches, Hubs, and How a Switch Learns
lessons.content:
## The box in the middle

In a real local network the devices are not wired directly to each other — they all plug into a box in the middle. The smart version of that box is a **switch**. A **switch** is a device with many ports (sockets) that **learns which device is on which port** and forwards each frame **only where it needs to go**.

Think of a smart mailroom. At first the mail clerk doesn't know where anyone sits. But every time a person hands in outgoing mail, the clerk notes their name and desk. Soon the clerk has a list and can carry each letter straight to the right desk instead of walking the whole floor.

## How a switch learns: MAC → port

The switch does exactly that, using MAC addresses. It keeps a **MAC address table** — a simple list of *which MAC address is reachable through which port*.

- When a frame **comes in** on a port, the switch reads the **source MAC** and records: "this MAC lives on this port." That is how it *learns*.
- When it needs to **send** a frame, it looks up the **destination MAC** in the table. If it knows the port, it forwards the frame out **only that one port**. If it has not learned that address yet, it sends the frame out **all** ports (just for that frame) to find the device.

So a switch **learns from the source address and forwards by the destination address**. Over a few moments it builds a full map of the network and cuts out wasted traffic.

## A hub: the dumb version

Before switches were cheap, networks used a **hub**. A hub is a dumb version of the same box: it does **no learning**. Every frame that comes in is **copied out to every other port**, whether or not the destination is there. That is wasteful and not private — every device sees every frame. A switch sends a frame only to the right port; a hub floods it everywhere.

## A bit of history: collisions and CSMA/CD

Old networks (and hubs) shared one wire, so only **one device could 'talk' at a time**. If two transmitted at once, their signals overlapped — a **collision** — and both had to stop and retry. The rulebook that managed this was called **CSMA/CD**: roughly, *listen first, and if you hear a collision, back off and try again later.*

Switches mostly made this history. Because a switch gives each device its own dedicated connection, devices can send and receive at the same time without colliding. So today CSMA/CD is mostly a story about how things used to work — worth knowing, but not something a modern switched network relies on.

## Recap

A **switch** learns which MAC address is on which port (learning from the source, forwarding by the destination) and sends each frame only where it belongs. A **hub** is the dumb older box that floods every frame to every port. Collisions and CSMA/CD were how shared-wire networks coped before switches made them mostly obsolete.

items.prompt: Compare the switch and hub in the diagram. What is the key difference shown?
choices: The switch floods the frame to every device; the hub sends it to only one
The switch forwards the frame to only the correct port; the hub copies it to every port
Both send the frame to every device equally
The hub learns MAC addresses and the switch does not
items.explanation: The diagram shows the switch sending the frame down only the one line that leads to the right device, using its learned MAC table, while the hub copies the same frame out to all of its ports. The switch is selective; the hub is not.

items.prompt: What does a switch do?
choices: It picks the best route for data across the whole Internet
It learns which device is on which port and forwards each frame only where it needs to go
It copies every frame to every port without thinking
It converts Wi-Fi signals into electricity
items.explanation: A switch is the smart box in the middle of a local network: it learns which MAC address is reachable on which port and forwards each frame only out the correct port. Copying every frame to every port is what a (dumb) hub does, and routing across the Internet is a higher-layer job.

items.prompt: How does a switch LEARN which device is on which port?
choices: It reads the source MAC of each incoming frame and records that MAC against the port it arrived on
It asks every device to type in its location
It reads the destination MAC and guesses the port
It is pre-programmed at the factory with every device's location
items.explanation: When a frame arrives, the switch looks at its source MAC (who sent it) and notes 'this MAC is on this port.' That builds the MAC address table over time. It forwards using the destination MAC, but it learns from the source MAC.

items.prompt: When a switch needs to send a frame and it already knows which port the destination MAC is on, what does it do?
choices: It floods the frame out of every port to be safe
It forwards the frame out only that one port
It drops the frame because it already knows the device
It sends the frame back out the port it came in on
items.explanation: If the destination MAC is in its table, the switch sends the frame out only the single port that leads to that device — efficient and private. Flooding to all ports only happens when the switch has *not* yet learned where the destination is.

items.prompt: What is the main difference between a hub and a switch?
choices: A hub learns MAC addresses; a switch does not
A hub copies every frame to every port; a switch forwards a frame only to the correct port
A hub is faster and smarter than a switch
A hub works only with Wi-Fi and a switch only with cables
items.explanation: A hub is the dumb box: it floods every incoming frame out all other ports with no learning. A switch learns where each device is and forwards each frame only to the right port. That makes switches faster, more private, and the modern default.

items.prompt: A switch receives a frame for a destination MAC it has NOT learned yet. What does it do?
choices: It throws the frame away because it doesn't know the device
It sends the frame out all ports (except the one it arrived on) to reach the device
It keeps the frame forever until the device sends something
It rewrites the destination MAC to one it knows
items.explanation: When the destination is unknown, the switch errs on the side of delivery: it floods the frame out every port except the ingress port. Whichever device is the real destination will respond, and the switch will then learn its port for next time.

items.prompt: The 'smart mailroom that learns everyone's desk' is an analogy for which device?
choices: A hub
A switch
A single network cable
A MAC address
items.explanation: The smart mailroom that notes each person's desk and then delivers letters straight to the right place is a switch — it learns which MAC is on which port and forwards accordingly. A hub would be a clerk who drops a copy of every letter on every desk.

items.prompt: What was a 'collision' on an old shared-wire network?
choices: Two devices transmitting at the same time so their signals overlapped and both had to retry
Two cables physically tangling together
A frame arriving with a bad FCS
A switch forwarding to the wrong port
items.explanation: On a shared wire only one device could transmit at a time. If two sent at once their signals overlapped — a collision — and both stopped and retried. This is unrelated to cables tangling, FCS errors, or switch forwarding mistakes.

items.prompt: Why is CSMA/CD mostly just history on modern networks?
choices: Because Wi-Fi made all cables obsolete
Because switches give each device its own connection, so devices can send and receive at once without colliding
Because frames no longer have a destination MAC
Because CSMA/CD was made illegal
items.explanation: CSMA/CD existed to manage collisions on a shared wire. A switch gives each device a dedicated link, so devices can transmit and receive simultaneously and collisions essentially disappear. CSMA/CD is still worth knowing as history, but switched networks don't depend on it.

items.prompt: In one phrase, a switch 'learns from the source and forwards by the destination.' What does that mean?
choices: It records where frames come FROM, but decides where to send them based on who they are FOR
It ignores both the source and destination addresses
It learns the destination and forwards by the source
It only works if every frame is a broadcast
items.explanation: A switch reads the *source* MAC of arriving frames to learn which device sits on which port, and it reads the *destination* MAC to decide which port to forward a frame out of. Source teaches it the map; destination tells it where this frame should go.

lessons.title: Broadcast vs Unicast, VLANs & Frame Size
lessons.content:
## Three ways to address a frame

A frame's destination MAC can aim at different numbers of devices. There are three flavours:

- **Unicast** — to **one** specific device. This is the everyday case: "this frame is for that laptop."
- **Broadcast** — to **every** device on the local network at once. There is a special all-devices address (every bit set to 1) that means "everyone here, listen up." It is like an announcement over a building's loudspeaker. Switches send a broadcast frame out all ports.
- **Multicast** — to a **specific group** of interested devices, not everyone and not just one. Like a memo sent only to the people who signed up for a particular newsletter.

Knowing these helps explain switch behaviour: a unicast goes to one port (once learned), while a broadcast is flooded to all ports because it is meant for everyone.

## Splitting one switch into VLANs

By default, every device plugged into one switch is on the same local network, and a broadcast reaches all of them. Sometimes you don't want that — say you want the staff computers separated from the guest Wi-Fi for security, even though they share one switch.

A **VLAN** (Virtual LAN, where LAN = Local Area Network) lets you **split one physical switch into separate virtual networks**. Ports in VLAN A behave as one local network; ports in VLAN B behave as a totally separate one. A broadcast in VLAN A never reaches VLAN B. To send data between two VLANs you need a **router** (the device that moves data between *different* networks — covered soon).

When VLANs need to span more than one switch, the link between the switches carries traffic for several VLANs at once. That shared link is called a **trunk**, and each frame on it gets a small **VLAN tag** added so the other switch knows which VLAN it belongs to. A normal port connected to a single device (in one VLAN) is called an **access port**.

## MTU and jumbo frames (brief)

Recall the payload tops out around 1500 bytes. That maximum is called the **MTU** (Maximum Transmission Unit) — the largest payload a frame may carry. Some specialized networks (like data centers) raise this to about 9000 bytes using **jumbo frames** to move bulk data more efficiently. The catch: **every device along the path must agree** on the larger size, or the oversized frames get dropped. For everyday networks, 1500 is the norm.

## Recap

Frames can be **unicast** (one device), **broadcast** (everyone), or **multicast** (a group). **VLANs** carve one physical switch into separate virtual networks that need a router to talk between them, with **trunks** and **VLAN tags** carrying multiple VLANs between switches. The **MTU** caps payload size at about 1500 bytes, which **jumbo frames** can raise to ~9000 when every device agrees.

items.prompt: In the VLAN diagram, two VLANs share one physical switch. What does the diagram show about them?
choices: Every device can freely talk to every other device
Devices within the same VLAN can talk to each other, but crossing between VLAN A and VLAN B needs a router
The two VLANs require two separate physical switches
VLANs make the switch forget all MAC addresses
items.explanation: The diagram shows one physical switch split into VLAN A and VLAN B. Devices inside the same VLAN communicate normally, but the two VLANs act as separate networks — crossing between them requires a router, even though they share one switch.

items.prompt: What does a UNICAST frame target?
choices: One specific device
Every device on the local network
A group of devices that signed up
No devices at all
items.explanation: Unicast means the frame is addressed to one specific device — the everyday case. 'Every device' is broadcast, and 'a group that signed up' is multicast.

items.prompt: What is a BROADCAST frame, and what does a switch do with it?
choices: A frame for one device; the switch sends it to one port
A frame for every device on the local network; the switch floods it out all ports
A frame with no destination; the switch drops it
A frame for a device on another network; the switch routes it
items.explanation: A broadcast uses the special all-devices address (every bit set to 1) and is meant for every device on the local network, so a switch floods it out all of its ports. It is like a loudspeaker announcement to the whole building.

items.prompt: How is MULTICAST different from broadcast?
choices: Multicast goes to a specific group of interested devices, not to absolutely everyone
Multicast goes to exactly one device
Multicast goes to devices on other networks only
There is no difference between multicast and broadcast
items.explanation: Multicast targets a particular group of devices that are interested (like a newsletter sent only to subscribers), whereas broadcast hits every device on the local network. Multicast is the in-between option: more than one device, but not all of them.

items.prompt: What is a VLAN?
choices: A faster type of network cable
A way to split one physical switch into separate virtual networks
A permanent hardware address like a MAC
The error-check at the end of a frame
items.explanation: A VLAN (Virtual LAN) divides one physical switch into multiple separate virtual networks. Ports in one VLAN behave as their own local network, isolated from the others, which is useful for separating, say, staff and guest traffic on the same hardware.

items.prompt: Two devices are on the same switch but in different VLANs. How can they communicate?
choices: They cannot communicate under any circumstances
Their traffic must go through a router, which moves data between different networks
They communicate directly because they share one switch
They must first be given the same MAC address
items.explanation: Different VLANs act as separate networks, so frames cannot pass directly between them even on one switch. A router (the device that moves data between different networks) is needed to bridge the two VLANs. Sharing a switch does not by itself let different VLANs talk.

items.prompt: What is a 'trunk' link, and why does it add a VLAN tag to frames?
choices: A link to a single device; the tag stores its MAC
A link between switches that carries several VLANs at once; the tag marks which VLAN each frame belongs to
A backup cable used only when the main one fails
A link to the Internet; the tag stores the website name
items.explanation: A trunk is a link (typically between switches) that carries traffic for multiple VLANs simultaneously. Because several VLANs share the link, each frame gets a small VLAN tag so the receiving switch knows which VLAN it belongs to. An access port, by contrast, connects a single device in one VLAN.

items.prompt: What does MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) mean for an Ethernet frame?
choices: The maximum number of devices on the network
The largest payload a frame may carry, normally about 1500 bytes
The minimum size a frame must be
The speed of the network link
items.explanation: The MTU is the largest payload that fits in one frame — normally about 1500 bytes on standard Ethernet. It is a size limit on the data per frame, not a device count, a minimum size, or a speed.

items.prompt: What is a jumbo frame, and what is the catch?
choices: A frame with no payload; the catch is it carries no data
A frame with a payload up to about 9000 bytes; the catch is every device on the path must agree on the larger size
A frame sent twice for safety; the catch is it doubles traffic
A frame with extra MAC addresses; the catch is it confuses switches
items.explanation: A jumbo frame raises the payload to roughly 9000 bytes to move bulk data more efficiently (common in data centers). The catch is that every device along the path must be set up for the larger size, or the oversized frames are simply dropped.

items.prompt: Why might someone put staff computers and guest Wi-Fi on different VLANs even though they share one switch?
