These running counts are how both sides stay in sync.

**3. Re-sending — retransmission.** If the sender does not get an ACK for a chunk within a reasonable time, it assumes that chunk was lost and **sends it again**. Nothing is silently skipped — this is the heart of TCP's reliability.

**4. Teardown — closing with FIN.** When a side is done, it sends a **FIN** ("finish") and the other side acknowledges it. Each direction is closed politely so no data in flight is dropped.

## Recap

TCP earns the word "reliable" by **setting up** a connection (SYN / SYN-ACK / ACK), **numbering** data so it can be ordered and acknowledged, **re-sending** anything that goes missing, and **closing** cleanly with FIN.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of the TCP 3-way handshake?
choices: To encrypt all data before it is sent
To let both sides agree to start talking and sync up their counting before sending real data
To pick the fastest route across the internet
To compress the data so it transfers faster
items.explanation: The handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) is how both sides say hello, confirm the other is there, and agree on starting sequence numbers before any real data flows. It is about setup and synchronization, not encryption, routing, or compression.

items.prompt: Which sequence of messages is the correct TCP 3-way handshake?
choices: ACK, then SYN, then FIN
SYN, then SYN-ACK, then ACK
FIN, then FIN-ACK, then RST
SYN-ACK, then ACK, then SYN
items.explanation: The order is SYN (start), SYN-ACK (yes, and here's my start), then ACK (got it). A handshake never begins with ACK or FIN; FIN messages are for closing, not opening.

items.prompt: In the handshake, what does the word SYN stand for, and what is it asking?
choices: Synchronize — 'let's start a connection and line up our counts'
System — 'restart the computer'
Send — 'here is the actual file data'
Sync — 'close this connection now'
items.explanation: SYN means synchronize: it opens the connection and shares the sender's starting sequence number so both sides can line up their counting. It is not the file data and is not a close request.

items.prompt: What does an acknowledgment (ACK) tell the sender?
choices: That the receiver wants to close the connection
Roughly, 'I have received the data up to here — send the next part'
That the data was encrypted successfully
That the sender should slow down to zero and stop
items.explanation: An ACK confirms received data and signals what the receiver expects next, keeping both sides in sync. It is not a close request and not an encryption confirmation.

items.prompt: Why does TCP attach a sequence number to each chunk of data?
choices: To encrypt each chunk with a different key
So the receiver can put chunks back in the correct order even if they arrive jumbled
To decide which Wi-Fi channel to use
To make each chunk smaller
items.explanation: Sequence numbers label the data so the receiver can reassemble it in order and notice anything missing — essential because IP can deliver chunks out of order. They are not about encryption, Wi-Fi, or size.

items.prompt: A sender transmits a chunk of data but never receives an acknowledgment for it. What does TCP do?
choices: Skips that chunk and moves on
Assumes it was lost and sends it again (retransmission)
Immediately closes the connection with a FIN
Encrypts the chunk and tries a different port
items.explanation: No ACK suggests the chunk was lost, so TCP retransmits it. This is the core of TCP's reliability — nothing is silently skipped. It does not close the connection just because one chunk went missing.

items.prompt: What does 'ordered delivery' mean in TCP, and how is it achieved?
choices: Data is delivered alphabetically; the app sorts it
The app receives bytes in the same order they were sent, because TCP reorders chunks using their sequence numbers
Data is delivered fastest-first regardless of order
Only one chunk is ever sent at a time, so order is automatic
items.explanation: TCP hands data to the application in the original send order. It uses sequence numbers to reorder any chunks that arrived out of order before delivering them. It is not alphabetical, and TCP can have many chunks in flight at once.

items.prompt: When a side is finished and wants to close a TCP connection gracefully, which message does it send?
choices: SYN
FIN
ACK only, forever
A second handshake
items.explanation: FIN ('finish') signals that a side is done sending; the other side acknowledges it, and each direction closes cleanly. SYN is for opening, not closing.

items.prompt: Why is closing a TCP connection done politely (FIN and an acknowledgment) instead of just cutting it off?
choices: To make sure data still in transit is not dropped and both sides agree it is over
Because the internet charges money per open connection
To re-encrypt the connection one last time
Because a connection cannot be reopened otherwise
items.explanation: A graceful close lets any data still in flight finish and confirms to both sides that the conversation is complete, so nothing is lost. It is about clean, lossless shutdown, not billing or encryption.

items.prompt: Put the life of a simple TCP connection in order.
choices: Send data → handshake → close
Handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK) → send and acknowledge data, re-sending anything lost → close with FIN
Close with FIN → handshake → send data
Send FIN → send SYN → send data
items.explanation: A connection opens with the 3-way handshake, then exchanges and acknowledges data (retransmitting losses), then closes with FIN. Setup always comes before data, and the close comes last.

items.prompt: Which single phrase best summarizes what TCP adds on top of IP's best-effort packets?
choices: A faster route with no setup time
A reliable, in-order stream of data with re-sending of anything lost
Encryption of every byte by default
A way to reach more than one computer at once
items.explanation: TCP turns unreliable packets into a reliable, ordered byte stream and retransmits losses. It is not inherently faster (setup and acknowledgments add overhead) and does not provide encryption by itself.

lessons.title: UDP: Bare and Fast, and When to Use It
lessons.content:
# UDP: As Simple As It Gets

If TCP is a careful, tracked delivery, **UDP** is the opposite: send the packet and move on. There is **no handshake**, **no acknowledgments**, **no re-sending**, and **no ordering**. UDP just stamps a small header on your data (mainly the source and destination port numbers) and hands it to IP. That bareness sounds like a weakness, but for the right jobs it is exactly the point.

## Build: why 'no guarantees' is sometimes perfect

Imagine a **live video call**. Each tiny slice of audio and video is only useful *right now*. If one slice is lost, re-sending it would deliver it too late — the conversation has already moved on — so it is better to skip it and keep going. TCP would stubbornly stop and re-send the missing slice, causing a freeze or lag. UDP just lets the lost slice go, and the call stays smooth. The same logic fits **online games** (your position a moment ago no longer matters), and **streaming** live media.

UDP is also great for **short request-and-reply** exchanges where setting up a whole TCP connection would be wasteful. The classic example is **DNS**, the system that looks up a name like example.com and returns its IP address. It is one tiny question and one tiny answer; UDP sends each in a single packet with no setup. Because UDP does no work to guarantee anything, *if* an app needs reliability it must add that itself — but many apps simply do not need it.

A quick word on **ports**: certain port numbers are **well-known** because they are reserved for common services so everyone can find them. A few to recognize: **80** for web traffic (HTTP), **443** for secure web (HTTPS), **53** for DNS lookups, and **22** for secure remote login (SSH).

## Recap

UDP is connectionless and unreliable on purpose: no setup, no re-sending, no ordering, just speed and a tiny header. That makes it ideal for **live voice/video, games, streaming, and quick lookups like DNS**, where being fast matters more than being perfect.

items.prompt: Which set of features describes UDP?
choices: Handshake, acknowledgments, re-sending, and ordering
No handshake, no acknowledgments, no re-sending, no ordering
Encryption and compression built in
Guaranteed delivery but no ordering
items.explanation: UDP is deliberately bare: it skips setup, acknowledgments, retransmission, and ordering. The first option describes TCP. UDP does not guarantee delivery at all.

items.prompt: TCP vs UDP: which is better for a live video call, and why?
choices: TCP, because re-sending every lost slice keeps the picture perfect
UDP, because a lost slice is better skipped than re-sent late, keeping the call smooth and low-latency
TCP, because it has a smaller header for video
UDP, because it guarantees slices arrive in order
items.explanation: In a live call, data is only useful immediately. UDP skips lost slices and keeps going, avoiding freezes; TCP would stall to re-send slices that are already too late to matter. (UDP does not guarantee order, ruling out the last choice.)

items.prompt: TCP vs UDP: which is better for downloading a file, and why?
choices: UDP, because losing a few bytes of a file is fine
TCP, because the whole file must arrive complete and in order, and TCP re-sends anything lost
UDP, because it sets up a connection to protect the file
TCP, because it never adds any overhead
items.explanation: A file is useless if bytes are missing or scrambled, so you want TCP's reliable, ordered delivery with retransmission. Losing bytes (the UDP behavior) would corrupt the file. TCP does add some overhead, but correctness is what matters here.

items.prompt: Why is UDP a good fit for DNS (looking up a name to get its IP address)?
choices: DNS transfers huge files that need ordering
DNS is one tiny question and one tiny answer, so skipping connection setup makes it fast
DNS must be encrypted, which only UDP allows
DNS needs guaranteed in-order delivery of many packets
items.explanation: A DNS exchange is usually a single small request and a single small reply. UDP sends each in one packet with no handshake overhead, which is quick. DNS is small, not a huge ordered transfer, and UDP does not provide encryption by itself.

items.prompt: If an app uses UDP but actually needs some reliability, what has to happen?
choices: UDP automatically turns into TCP
The application itself must add whatever reliability it needs, because UDP provides none
The router adds the missing acknowledgments
Nothing — UDP quietly re-sends lost packets anyway
items.explanation: UDP gives no guarantees, so any reliability (such as detecting and re-requesting lost data) must be built into the application. UDP never silently becomes TCP and does not re-send on its own.

items.prompt: Which port number is well-known for secure web traffic (HTTPS)?
items.explanation: 443 is the well-known port for HTTPS (secure web). 22 is SSH (secure login), 53 is DNS, and 25 is email transfer. Well-known ports let clients find common services at predictable numbers.

items.prompt: What does it mean that ports 80, 443, 53, and 22 are 'well-known'?
choices: They are randomly chosen each time a program starts
They are reserved for common services so clients always know where to find them
They are the only ports that exist
They are reserved for the fastest computers
items.explanation: Well-known ports are reserved for standard services (web, secure web, DNS, SSH) so a client can connect without guessing. Ports are not randomly assigned for these services, and there are tens of thousands of port numbers in total.

items.prompt: An online multiplayer game sends your character's position many times per second. Why is UDP usually the better choice?
choices: Because old position updates are useless once a newer one exists, so re-sending lost ones would only add delay
Because UDP guarantees every position arrives in order
Because UDP encrypts the game data automatically
Because TCP cannot be used for games at all
items.explanation: Fresh updates replace old ones, so a lost update is best ignored rather than re-sent late. UDP's no-re-send, low-latency behavior fits this. UDP does not guarantee order or add encryption, and TCP is technically usable but a worse fit here.

items.prompt: Compared with TCP, the UDP header is best described as:
choices: Much larger, to hold acknowledgment and ordering information
Very small, mainly just the source and destination port numbers
The same size, since both do the same work
Empty — UDP has no header at all
items.explanation: Because UDP does so little, its header is tiny — essentially the port numbers (plus a length and a checksum). TCP needs more header space for sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and control flags. UDP does have a small header; it is not absent.

items.prompt: Which job is the WORST fit for UDP?
choices: A live voice call
Transferring a bank statement PDF that must be byte-perfect
A fast DNS name lookup
Streaming a live sports broadcast
items.explanation: A byte-perfect file transfer needs reliable, ordered delivery — TCP's strength — because any lost or scrambled bytes corrupt it. Live calls, DNS lookups, and live streaming all tolerate occasional loss in exchange for speed, which suits UDP.

lessons.title: Not Overwhelming the Receiver or the Network (plus a Peek at QUIC)
lessons.content:
# Going Fast Without Causing a Pile-Up

TCP does more than re-send lost data. It also paces itself so it does not flood two things that have limits: the **receiver** at the other end, and the **network** in between. These two ideas — **flow control** and **congestion control** — are the difference between a smooth transfer and a stuttering one. We will keep them at the level of plain ideas (no formulas).

## Build: two kinds of 'don't overwhelm'

**Flow control — don't overwhelm the *receiver*.** The receiving computer has a limited buffer (a holding area) for incoming data. If the sender blasts data faster than the receiver can process it, that buffer overflows and data is lost. So the receiver tells the sender how much room it has left — a number called the **window**. The sender keeps the amount of unconfirmed data within that window. Think of it as the receiver saying "I can take this much more right now"; as it works through the data, the window opens up again.

**Congestion control — don't overwhelm the *network*.** Even if the receiver is fast, the links and routers between you have limits. Flooding them causes packets to be dropped. TCP cannot see the whole network, so it probes carefully. It begins by sending slowly and **speeding up** as acknowledgments come back smoothly — an approach called **slow start**. When a packet is lost (a hint the network is full), TCP **backs off**, slowing down, then cautiously climbs again. This gentle rise-and-back-off keeps the network busy without melting it.

## A peek ahead: head-of-line blocking and QUIC

Because TCP delivers one strictly ordered stream, a single lost chunk makes everything behind it wait until that chunk is re-sent — even unrelated data is stuck. This stall is called **head-of-line blocking** (the front of the line holds up the rest). A newer protocol, **QUIC**, was built to avoid it. Cleverly, QUIC runs on top of **UDP** and rebuilds reliability and ordering itself, but in a way that lets independent streams flow without blocking each other. You will meet QUIC in detail in a later sub-topic; for now, just know that modern protocols can rebuild TCP-like reliability on UDP.

## Recap

**Flow control** uses a **window** so the sender does not overwhelm the **receiver**; **congestion control** (start slow, speed up, back off on loss) keeps the sender from overwhelming the **network**. And because strict ordering can cause head-of-line blocking, newer protocols like **QUIC** rebuild reliability on top of UDP to avoid it.

items.prompt: What problem does flow control solve?
choices: It stops the sender from overwhelming the receiver's limited buffer
It chooses the shortest route across the internet
It encrypts the connection end to end
It assigns port numbers to each app
items.explanation: Flow control matches the sender's pace to what the receiver can handle, so the receiver's buffer does not overflow and lose data. It is not about routing, encryption, or ports.

items.prompt: In flow control, what does the 'window' represent?
choices: The time of day the connection is allowed to run
How much more data the receiver currently has room to accept
The number of routers between sender and receiver
The encryption strength of the connection
items.explanation: The window is the receiver's advertised free buffer space — 'I can take this much more right now.' The sender keeps unconfirmed data within it. It is not a time limit, a hop count, or an encryption setting.

items.prompt: What problem does congestion control solve?
choices: It stops the sender from overwhelming the network between the two computers
It guarantees the data is encrypted
It decides which program receives the data
It makes IP addresses shorter
items.explanation: Congestion control protects the shared network (links and routers) from being flooded, which would cause dropped packets. Choosing the program is what ports do; it is unrelated to encryption or address length.

items.prompt: What is the main difference between flow control and congestion control?
choices: Flow control protects the receiver; congestion control protects the network
Flow control protects the network; congestion control protects the receiver
They are two names for the same thing
Flow control encrypts data; congestion control compresses it
items.explanation: Flow control is about not overwhelming the receiving computer; congestion control is about not overwhelming the network in between. They target two different bottlenecks, and neither does encryption or compression.

items.prompt: What does 'slow start' mean in congestion control?
choices: The connection is permanently capped at a slow speed
TCP begins by sending slowly and speeds up as acknowledgments return smoothly
The receiver slowly reads the data to save power
The handshake is performed very slowly
items.explanation: Slow start means TCP starts cautious and ramps up as it sees that data is getting through. It is not a permanent cap, nor about the receiver's reading speed or the handshake timing.

items.prompt: In congestion control, why does packet loss cause TCP to slow down?
choices: Loss means the receiver wants to close the connection
Loss is a hint the network may be full, so backing off helps relieve the overload
Loss means the data was encrypted incorrectly
Loss tells TCP to switch to UDP
items.explanation: Dropped packets often signal that links or routers are overloaded. TCP treats loss as that hint and reduces its sending rate to ease the congestion, then climbs again. Loss is not a close request or an encryption error.

items.prompt: A receiver advertises a smaller and smaller window as a transfer goes on. What is most likely happening?
choices: The network routers are rebooting
The receiver is filling up faster than it can process the data, so it has less free buffer room
The sender ran out of data to send
The connection is being encrypted more strongly
items.explanation: A shrinking window means the receiver's free buffer space is decreasing — it is not keeping up. Flow control then tells the sender to ease off so nothing overflows. It is not about routers, running out of data, or encryption.

items.prompt: What is 'head-of-line blocking' in TCP?
choices: When the fastest data is always delivered first
When one lost chunk forces all the data behind it to wait until that chunk is re-sent
When two computers try to connect to the same port
When the receiver's window drops to zero on purpose
items.explanation: Because TCP delivers one strictly ordered stream, a single missing chunk holds up everything after it until it is retransmitted — like one stalled car blocking the whole lane. It is not about speed ordering or port collisions.

items.prompt: How does QUIC relate to UDP?
choices: QUIC replaces UDP with a brand-new lower layer
QUIC runs on top of UDP and rebuilds reliability and ordering itself, while avoiding head-of-line blocking
QUIC is just another name for TCP
QUIC removes the need for any port numbers
items.explanation: QUIC is built on UDP's bare delivery and adds back reliable, ordered streams — but in a way that keeps independent streams from blocking each other. It does not replace UDP, is not TCP, and still uses ports.

items.prompt: Why would designers build a new protocol like QUIC on top of UDP rather than on TCP?
choices: Because UDP already guarantees ordering they can reuse
Because UDP's bare, no-ordering delivery lets QUIC add its own smarter reliability without TCP's head-of-line blocking
Because UDP is encrypted and TCP is not
Because TCP cannot be used on the internet anymore
items.explanation: UDP imposes no ordering, so QUIC is free to build independent streams that do not block one another — sidestepping TCP's head-of-line blocking. UDP does not provide ordering or built-in encryption, and TCP is still widely used.

items.prompt: Which pairing correctly matches each control to what it protects?
choices: Flow control → the network; congestion control → the receiver
Flow control → the receiver (via the window); congestion control → the network (start slow, back off on loss)
Both protect only the sender
Flow control → encryption; congestion control → routing
items.explanation: Flow control uses the receiver's window to avoid overwhelming the receiver; congestion control starts slow and backs off on loss to avoid overwhelming the network. The other options swap or misattribute these roles.

lessons.title: Why We Need TLS: The Open Network Problem
lessons.content:
## The problem: networks are open by default

You have used **HTTPS** and seen the little **padlock** in your browser. This lesson explains what that padlock actually buys you, starting from the problem it solves.

Here is the uncomfortable truth: by default, data on a network travels **in the open**. When your laptop sends a message to a website, that message hops across cables, routers, and Wi-Fi on its way. Anyone sitting *in between* -- a snoop on the coffee-shop Wi-Fi, or your **ISP** (Internet Service Provider, the company that connects you to the internet) -- could read it, or even quietly change it. Plain text on a network is like a **postcard**: every mail handler along the way can read it.

## Three things can go wrong

On an open network, an attacker in the middle could:

- **Eavesdrop** -- read your private data (passwords, messages) as it passes by.
- **Tamper** -- change the data in transit, so you receive something different from what was sent.
- **Impersonate** -- pretend to be the bank you think you are talking to, and trick you into handing over secrets.

## The fix: TLS wraps the connection in a secure tunnel

**TLS (Transport Layer Security)** is the technology that wraps a normal connection in a secure **tunnel**. It is the **"S" in HTTPS** and the reason your browser shows a padlock. It sits on top of the ordinary connection (TCP) and protects whatever travels through it.

TLS gives you **three guarantees**, each one defeating a threat above:

- **Encryption** (also called *confidentiality*): the data is scrambled, so an eavesdropper sees only meaningless bytes. Think of **sealing a letter in an envelope** instead of mailing a postcard.
- **Integrity**: if anyone changes the data along the way, the change is **detected** and the connection is rejected -- like a **tamper-evident seal** on a package.
- **Authentication**: you can be sure you are really talking to the **real** website (the real bank), not an impostor. This is proven by a **certificate**, which works like checking someone's **passport or ID** before you trust them.

A handy memory aid: TLS protects you from **eavesdropping** (with encryption), **tampering** (with integrity), and **impersonation** (with authentication).

## Where TLS sits: HTTPS = HTTP over TLS

The web normally speaks **HTTP** (HyperText Transfer Protocol -- the language of web requests like "get me this page"). **HTTPS** is simply **HTTP running inside a TLS tunnel**. Same web request you already know, now wrapped so it is private and trustworthy. The padlock icon just means "this page came over TLS."

## Recap

Networks are open by default, so data can be eavesdropped, tampered with, or sent to an impostor. TLS wraps the connection in a secure tunnel and provides three guarantees -- **encryption, integrity, and authentication**. HTTPS is just HTTP carried over TLS, and the padlock is its sign.

items.prompt: Why is data on a network said to travel "in the open" by default?
choices: Anyone positioned in between (like a Wi-Fi snoop or your ISP) could read or change it
The data is automatically deleted after it is sent
Only the sender and receiver can ever see it, with no exceptions
It can only travel over Wi-Fi, never over cables
items.explanation: Without protection, a message passes through cables, routers, and Wi-Fi where parties in the middle can read or alter it -- like a postcard every handler can read. The other options are wrong: the data is not auto-deleted, it is NOT private by default (that is the whole problem), and it travels over both cables and Wi-Fi.

items.prompt: What are the THREE things TLS provides?
choices: Encryption, integrity, and authentication
Speed, compression, and caching
A faster Wi-Fi signal, more range, and less battery use
A username, a password, and a backup
items.explanation: TLS gives encryption (eavesdroppers see only scramble), integrity (tampering is detected), and authentication (you are really talking to the real site). The other options describe performance features or login mechanics, which are not what TLS guarantees.

items.prompt: Which TLS guarantee stops an eavesdropper from reading your data?
choices: Encryption (confidentiality) -- the data is scrambled into meaningless bytes
Integrity -- it detects changes
Authentication -- it proves identity
Compression -- it shrinks the data
items.explanation: Encryption scrambles the data so a snoop sees only gibberish; that is what defeats eavesdropping. Integrity catches tampering and authentication proves who you are talking to -- both important, but neither is what hides the contents. Compression has nothing to do with secrecy.

items.prompt: An attacker in the middle quietly changes a few bytes of your message as it passes. Which TLS guarantee is designed to catch this?
choices: Integrity -- a change in transit is detected and the connection is rejected
Encryption -- it prevents anyone from changing the data at all
Authentication -- it speeds up the connection
Availability -- it keeps the server online
items.explanation: Integrity is the guarantee that detects tampering, like a tamper-evident seal: if bytes are altered, TLS notices and rejects them. Encryption hides the data but does not by itself promise it was unchanged; that is a separate job. Authentication is about identity, and availability is unrelated.

items.prompt: What does HTTPS actually stand for, in terms of how it is built?
choices: HTTP carried inside a TLS tunnel (HTTP over TLS)
A completely different web language that replaced HTTP
HTTP sent twice for reliability
HTTP with the images removed to save data
items.explanation: HTTPS is just ordinary HTTP running inside a TLS tunnel -- the 'S' is the TLS layer. It is not a new language, not HTTP duplicated, and not about stripping images. The web request you know is simply wrapped to make it private and trustworthy.

items.prompt: The padlock icon in your browser's address bar means what?
choices: The connection to this site is protected by TLS
The website is guaranteed to be a good company you can trust completely
Your device has antivirus software installed
The page will load faster than other pages
items.explanation: The padlock means the page came over TLS, so the connection is encrypted, tamper-checked, and the site proved its identity. It does NOT vouch that the business is honest -- a scam site can still use TLS. It says nothing about antivirus or page speed.

items.prompt: Which everyday object is the best analogy for TLS encryption?
choices: Sealing a letter in an envelope only the recipient can open, instead of mailing a postcard
Writing in pencil so you can erase it
Mailing a postcard that everyone along the way can read
Speaking louder so more people hear you
items.explanation: Encryption is like a sealed envelope: only the intended recipient can read the contents. A postcard is the OPPOSITE -- it is what an unprotected (plain HTTP) connection is like, readable by everyone in between. Pencil and speaking louder do not capture privacy.

items.prompt: Without TLS, which of these is a real risk on a public coffee-shop Wi-Fi?
choices: Someone on the same network could read the password you type into a plain-HTTP site
Nothing, because Wi-Fi passwords always encrypt everything end to end
Your laptop would refuse to connect to any website
The website would automatically switch itself to HTTPS for you
items.explanation: On an open or shared network, traffic to a plain-HTTP site can be read by others in between, including passwords. A Wi-Fi password only protects the local radio link, not the whole path to the server, and sites do not automatically upgrade for you. This is exactly why TLS exists.

items.prompt: Which TLS guarantee makes sure you are talking to the REAL bank and not an impostor?
choices: Authentication -- proven by a certificate, like checking an ID
Encryption -- which scrambles the data
Integrity -- which detects edits
Compression -- which shrinks the data
items.explanation: Authentication proves the server's identity using a certificate, much like checking a passport before trusting someone. Encryption and integrity protect the data itself but do not, on their own, prove WHO is on the other end. Compression is unrelated to security.

lessons.title: Two Kinds of Keys, and Why TLS Uses Both
lessons.content:
## The puzzle at the heart of secure connections

TLS needs to scramble (encrypt) your data so a snoop cannot read it. To scramble and unscramble, both sides need a shared secret -- a **key**. But here is the puzzle: how do you and a website agree on a secret key when everything you send travels over an **open** line a snoop is already watching? You cannot just send the key in the clear; the snoop would grab it.

The answer is that there are **two different kinds of keys**, with different strengths, and TLS cleverly uses both.

## Idea 1: Symmetric keys (one shared secret)

A **symmetric key** is a single shared secret that both **locks** (encrypts) and **unlocks** (decrypts). It is like a **house key** two people both own: the same key locks and opens the door.

- **Strength: it is very fast.** Symmetric encryption can scramble huge amounts of data quickly.
- **Weakness: both sides must already share the same secret.** And we are back to the puzzle -- how do you both get the same key over an open line without a snoop copying it?

## Idea 2: Public-key (asymmetric) keys -- a lock-and-key PAIR

A **public/private key pair** (also called **asymmetric** crypto) is the clever fix. Instead of one key, you get **two matched keys** that work together:

- A **public key**, which acts like a **padlock anyone can snap shut**. You can hand it out to the whole world.
- A **private key**, which is the **only** thing that can open what that padlock closed. You keep it secret and never share it.

The magic: anyone can lock a message using your public padlock, but **only you**, holding the private key, can unlock it. So someone can send you a secret over a wide-open line -- they lock it with your public key, and even a snoop who sees everything cannot open it.

- **Strength: it solves the puzzle** -- two strangers can agree on a secret over an open line.
- **Weakness: it is slow,** so you would not want to encrypt all your data this way.

## How TLS combines them (the hybrid trick)

Notice the strengths are complementary: symmetric is **fast but hard to set up**; public-key is **easy to set up but slow**. So TLS uses each for what it is good at, in two phases:

1. **Set-up phase (public-key):** the two sides use the slow public/private key idea to **safely agree on a brand-new shared secret** over the open line. A snoop watching cannot learn it.
2. **Bulk phase (symmetric):** they switch to that fast shared **symmetric** key and use it to encrypt all the real data for the rest of the session.

This two-step combination is sometimes called a **hybrid** approach: public-key to agree on the secret, symmetric to do the heavy lifting. You get the best of both -- a secret agreed safely over an open line, then fast encryption afterward.

## Recap

A **symmetric** key is one shared secret (fast, but hard to share safely). A **public-key pair** is a public lock plus a private key (solves sharing a secret over an open line, but slow). TLS uses **public-key to agree on a shared symmetric key, then switches to the fast symmetric key** for the bulk of the data.

items.prompt: What is a symmetric key?
choices: A single shared secret that both encrypts and decrypts the data
A pair of keys, one public and one private
A key that only the server is allowed to have
A password you type into a website
items.explanation: Symmetric means one shared secret used for both locking and unlocking, like a house key two people copy. A public/private pair is the OTHER kind (asymmetric). It is shared by both sides, not server-only, and it is not the same as a login password.

items.prompt: In a public/private key pair, who can open a message that was locked with the PUBLIC key?
choices: Only the holder of the matching private key
Anyone, because the public key is public
Only someone with the same public key
Nobody -- once locked it can never be opened
items.explanation: The public key is like a padlock anyone can snap shut, but only the matching private key opens it. That asymmetry is the whole point. If the public key could also unlock, there would be no secrecy. And it certainly can be opened -- by the private-key holder.

items.prompt: What is the MAIN problem with symmetric keys on their own?
choices: Both sides must somehow agree on the same secret over an open line first
They are far too slow to encrypt real data
They can only be used once and then thrown away
They reveal who you are talking to
items.explanation: Symmetric keys are fast, but both parties need the SAME secret, and sharing it over an open line risks a snoop copying it. That sharing problem is exactly what public-key crypto solves. Symmetric is fast (not slow), is not inherently single-use, and does not reveal identities.

items.prompt: Symmetric vs public-key: what is the key difference, and why does TLS use BOTH?
choices: Symmetric is one fast shared key but hard to share safely; public-key is a slow public/private pair that lets you agree on a secret over an open line -- so TLS uses public-key to agree on a key, then symmetric for speed
Symmetric is more secure, so TLS uses only symmetric and never public-key
Public-key is faster, so TLS uses it for all the data and symmetric only for login
They are the same thing with different names, and TLS picks one at random
items.explanation: The two have complementary strengths: symmetric is fast but hard to set up safely; public-key is easy to set up over an open line but slow. TLS uses public-key to safely agree on a shared symmetric key, then switches to the fast symmetric key for the bulk of the data. They are NOT the same, and the speeds are the reverse of options 2 and 3.

items.prompt: Why does TLS NOT just use public-key encryption for everything?
choices: Public-key crypto is slow, so it is impractical for large amounts of data
Public-key crypto is not secure enough to use at all
Public-key crypto cannot be used over the internet
Browsers do not support public-key crypto
items.explanation: Public-key operations are comparatively slow, so using them for all data would be sluggish. Instead TLS uses public-key just once to set up a fast shared key. Public-key crypto is secure, works fine over the internet, and is fully supported by browsers; speed is the reason.

items.prompt: In the hybrid approach, what is the role of the public/private key pair?
choices: To safely agree on a shared symmetric secret over the open line
To encrypt every byte of the actual web page
To compress the data before sending
To store your passwords on the server
items.explanation: The public/private pair is used in the set-up phase only: to agree on a fresh shared secret without a snoop learning it. The bulk data is then encrypted with the fast symmetric key, not the key pair. It is not for compression or password storage.

items.prompt: A public key is best compared to which everyday object?
choices: An open padlock you can hand out, that only your private key can re-open once snapped shut
A house key that both locks and unlocks the same door
A secret password you must keep hidden
A photo ID card
items.explanation: A public key works like a padlock anyone can snap shut, while only the private key reopens it -- so it is safe to share widely. The house key that does both is the SYMMETRIC analogy. A public key is meant to be shared (unlike a secret password), and an ID card is the analogy for a certificate, not a key.

items.prompt: After the set-up phase, which key does TLS use to encrypt the bulk of your data, and why?
choices: The shared symmetric key, because it is fast
The server's public key, because it is fast
Your private key, because it is most secret
A brand-new public/private pair for every byte
items.explanation: Once both sides share a symmetric key, TLS switches to it for all the real data precisely because symmetric encryption is fast. Public-key crypto is slow and is only used to set up that shared key. Making a new key pair per byte would be absurdly slow.

items.prompt: How can two strangers agree on a secret key even though a snoop sees every message they exchange?
choices: By using public-key crypto: one side locks the secret with the other's public key, and only the matching private key can open it
By sending the secret key as plain text and hoping the snoop is not looking
By both choosing the same key by luck
It is impossible; a snoop can always learn the key
items.explanation: Public-key crypto solves exactly this: a secret locked with the recipient's public key can only be opened by their private key, so a watching snoop learns nothing. Sending it as plain text would expose it, matching keys by luck is hopeless, and it is very much possible -- that is the breakthrough TLS relies on.

lessons.title: Certificates & Certificate Authorities: The Chain of Trust
lessons.content:
## A missing piece: who are you really talking to?

In the last lesson, the public/private key trick let two sides agree on a secret over an open line. But there is a gap. When a website hands you a public key and says "lock your secret with this," how do you know that key really belongs to **bank.com** -- and not to an **impostor** who jumped in the middle and handed you *their* public key instead? If you trust the wrong key, you would happily set up a secure tunnel... straight to the attacker.

This is the **authentication** problem from Lesson 1. The solution is a **certificate**.

## What a certificate is

A **certificate** is a small digital document that says, in effect: **"this public key belongs to bank.com."** It bundles together the site's name (the domain), its public key, and an expiry date. Think of it as an **ID card for a website**.

But an ID card is only useful if you trust whoever issued it. Anyone can print a fake badge that says "I am the bank." So a certificate is only convincing because it is **vouched for by a trusted third party**.

## Certificate Authorities (CAs)

That trusted third party is a **Certificate Authority (CA)** -- a well-known organization whose entire job is to verify identities and then **digitally sign** certificates. When a CA signs bank.com's certificate, it is putting its reputation behind the statement: **"we checked, and this public key really does belong to bank.com."**

It is like a **passport**: you trust a passport not because the traveler printed it, but because a **government you trust** issued and stamped it. The CA is the government office of the web.

## The chain of trust

In practice, trust is passed down a short **chain**:

- At the top is a **root CA**, whose own certificate is **pre-installed** in your browser and operating system. These roots are the handful of authorities your device trusts from the start.
- A root usually signs an **intermediate CA**.
- The intermediate signs the actual **site certificate** (bank.com).

So the trust flows **root -> intermediate -> site**. Your browser starts at the site certificate and walks **up** the chain, checking each signature, until it reaches a root it already trusts. If the chain connects to a trusted root, the identity is accepted.

## What your browser checks

Before showing the padlock, your browser validates the certificate by asking:

1. **Does the name match?** The certificate must be for the exact site you visited (bank.com), not some other name.
2. **Is it still valid (not expired)?** Certificates have an expiry date and must be current.
3. **Does the chain reach a trusted root?** Following root -> intermediate -> site, does it end at a root your device trusts?
4. **Has it been revoked?** Occasionally a certificate is cancelled early (for example, if its private key was stolen); the browser checks it is not on a cancelled list.

If every check passes, you get the padlock. If any check fails, the browser warns you instead -- which is the subject of the next lesson.

## Recap

A **certificate** is a signed document proving a public key belongs to a specific website -- a website's ID card. A **Certificate Authority (CA)** is the trusted third party that vouches for it, like a government issuing a passport. Trust flows down a **chain** from a pre-trusted **root** through intermediates to the site, and your browser walks that chain (checking name, expiry, chain, and revocation) before showing the padlock.

items.prompt: What does a certificate prove?
choices: That a particular public key really belongs to a particular website (its identity)
That the website's data has been compressed
That the connection is using the fastest possible speed
That you have an account on the website
items.explanation: A certificate binds a public key to a website's name -- it is the website's ID card, proving the key belongs to that site. It says nothing about compression, speed, or whether you personally have an account there.

items.prompt: What is a Certificate Authority (CA)?
choices: A trusted third party that verifies identities and signs certificates to vouch for them
A government agency that controls the entire internet
The company that sells you internet access
A piece of software that scans for viruses
items.explanation: A CA is a trusted organization that checks who someone is and then digitally signs their certificate, vouching that the public key belongs to that site -- like a passport office. It is not a controller of the internet, not your ISP, and not antivirus software.

items.prompt: What does a certificate AND its signing CA together prove to your browser?
choices: That a trusted authority has vouched the public key truly belongs to this website, so you are not talking to an impostor
That the website's business is honest and its prices are fair
That the website will never go offline
That your data will be delivered faster
items.explanation: A certificate states 'this key belongs to bank.com,' and a CA's signature means a trusted authority checked and vouches for that claim -- so an impostor cannot fake the real site's identity. It does NOT certify the business is honest, guarantee uptime, or affect speed.

items.prompt: In the chain of trust, what is special about a ROOT CA?
choices: Its certificate is pre-installed and trusted by your browser/operating system from the start
It is the website you are actually visiting
It changes every time you load a page
It is chosen by you each time you connect
items.explanation: Root CAs are the handful of authorities whose certificates ship pre-installed in your device, forming the anchor of trust. The site certificate sits at the BOTTOM of the chain, not the top. Roots do not change per page and are not picked by the user.

items.prompt: A certificate is most like which everyday object?
choices: A passport: trusted because a trusted authority issued and stamped it, not because you printed it yourself
A house key that locks and unlocks a door
A postcard anyone can read
A receipt for a purchase
items.explanation: A certificate is like a passport -- it proves identity because a trusted authority (the CA) issued it, just as a government issues a passport. A house key is the symmetric-key analogy, a postcard represents unprotected data, and a receipt is unrelated.

items.prompt: Which direction does your browser walk the chain of trust to validate a site?
choices: From the site certificate up through any intermediates until it reaches a trusted root
From the root downward, ignoring the site certificate
It checks only the site certificate and nothing above it
It picks one certificate in the chain at random to check
items.explanation: The browser starts at the site (leaf) certificate and follows the signatures upward through intermediates until it reaches a root it already trusts. Checking only the leaf, or just one random link, would not establish trust; the whole point is connecting the site up to a pre-trusted root.

items.prompt: Which of these is one of the checks your browser performs on a certificate?
choices: That the certificate's name matches the site you actually visited
That the website loads in under one second
That you have visited the site before
That the site uses a popular programming language
items.explanation: Name match is a core check: the certificate must be for the exact site you visited (other checks are expiry, chain-to-a-trusted-root, and revocation). Load speed, prior visits, and the site's programming language are not part of certificate validation.

items.prompt: Why is it not enough for a website to simply present a public key on its own, without a certificate?
choices: An impostor in the middle could hand you their own public key, and you would have no way to know it is not the real site's
Public keys are too large to send without a certificate
A public key cannot be used for encryption
Browsers cannot read a public key unless it is compressed
items.explanation: A bare public key carries no proof of ownership, so an attacker could substitute their own and you would unknowingly secure a tunnel to them. The certificate, signed by a trusted CA, is what proves the key belongs to the real site. Size, encryption ability, and compression are not the issue.

items.prompt: A typical chain might be root -> intermediate -> site. What does the intermediate CA do?
choices: It is signed by the root and in turn signs the site's certificate, passing trust down the chain
It is the site you are visiting
It replaces the need for a root CA entirely
It is the symmetric key used for encryption
items.explanation: The intermediate sits between the root and the site: the root signs the intermediate, and the intermediate signs the site certificate, so trust flows root -> intermediate -> site. It is not the site itself, it still relies on the root above it, and it is a certificate, not an encryption key.

lessons.title: The Handshake, TLS 1.3, and Certificate Warnings
lessons.content:
## Putting it together: the handshake as a story

You now know the pieces -- a secure tunnel, two kinds of keys, and certificates. The moment they all come together, right when a secure connection begins, is called the **handshake**. It is a quick back-and-forth where two sides agree on how to talk securely, *before* any real data flows. Told as a plain story, it goes:

1. **"Hello."** Your browser says hello and lists what kinds of encryption it supports.
2. **"Hello -- here is my certificate."** The server replies and sends its **certificate**. Your browser checks it (name, expiry, chain to a trusted root, not revoked) to confirm the server is really who it claims to be. This is **authentication** happening.
3. **"Let's agree on keys."** Using the public/private key idea, the two sides agree on a shared **symmetric** key over the open line, without a snoop learning it.
4. **"Now we're encrypted."** From here on, everything is scrambled with that fast shared key. The real conversation (your web requests) begins, safely.

That is the whole idea: **hello -> server proves its identity with a certificate -> agree on keys -> encrypted**.

## TLS 1.3 is the faster, modern version

TLS has improved over the years. The current version, **TLS 1.3**, is **faster** than older versions: it completes the handshake in fewer round trips, so secure pages start loading sooner. It also drops a lot of old, weak options, keeping only strong, modern ones. You do not need the internal details -- just know that **TLS 1.3 = the modern, leaner, faster handshake**, and it is what most secure sites use today.

## Forward secrecy, in one line

Modern TLS provides **forward secrecy**: it uses **fresh keys for every session**, so even if an attacker records your encrypted traffic today and *later* steals the server's long-term private key, they **still cannot unlock those old recordings**. Each conversation's key is thrown away when it ends.

## When something is wrong: certificate warnings

Sometimes your browser interrupts with a scary full-page **certificate warning** instead of the padlock. That means one of the validation checks **failed**. The common reasons map directly to the checks from the last lesson:

- **Expired certificate** -- the certificate's valid-until date has passed. Very common; the site owner simply forgot to renew it.
- **Self-signed certificate** -- the certificate was not signed by a trusted CA, only by the site itself. It is like a homemade ID card no trusted authority vouches for, so your browser cannot confirm the identity.
- **Name mismatch** -- the certificate is for a different name than the site you visited (for example, a certificate for `example.com` served on `shop.example.com`). The identity does not line up.

These warnings are **protecting you**: they mean "I could not safely confirm who you are talking to." On a site handling anything sensitive, you should not click through them.

## TLS is not the only tool: defense in depth

TLS secures a single connection (one browser to one website). But security usually comes in **layers** -- an idea called **defense in depth**, meaning you do not rely on a single protection. For example, a **VPN (Virtual Private Network)** wraps *all* of your device's traffic in an encrypted tunnel to a trusted point, useful on untrusted Wi-Fi; **IPsec** is a common technology for building such tunnels between whole networks. TLS, VPNs, and other safeguards stack together so that if one layer is bypassed, others still protect you.

## Recap

The **handshake** is the opening exchange: hello, the server proves identity with its **certificate**, both sides agree on keys, then everything is encrypted. **TLS 1.3** is the faster modern version, and **forward secrecy** means old recordings stay safe even if a key is stolen later. **Certificate warnings** (expired, self-signed, name mismatch) mean a safety check failed -- heed them. And TLS is one layer among many in **defense in depth** (alongside tools like VPNs/IPsec).

items.prompt: What is the TLS handshake?
choices: The opening back-and-forth where two sides agree on how to talk securely, before any real data flows
The moment you type your password into a website
The encryption of a single video frame
A physical cable connection between two computers
items.explanation: The handshake is the setup conversation at the start of a secure connection: the sides greet, prove identity, and agree on keys before exchanging real data. It is not about typing a password, encrypting one frame, or any physical cabling.

items.prompt: In the handshake story, how does the server prove it is really who it claims to be?
choices: It sends its certificate, which the browser checks against a trusted CA
It sends its private key for the browser to inspect
It asks the browser to trust it without any proof
It sends the user's password back
items.explanation: The server presents its certificate, and the browser validates it (name, expiry, chain to a trusted root). A server must NEVER send its private key -- that would destroy its security. Blind trust and echoing passwords are not how identity is proven.

items.prompt: What is the correct order of the handshake story?
choices: Hello -> server proves identity with its certificate -> agree on keys -> encrypted data
Encrypted data -> hello -> agree on keys -> certificate
Agree on keys -> encrypted data -> hello -> certificate
Certificate -> encrypted data -> hello -> agree on keys
items.explanation: First the sides say hello, then the server proves its identity with a certificate, then they agree on a shared key, and only THEN does encrypted real data flow. You cannot encrypt before agreeing on keys, which rules out the other orderings.

items.prompt: What is the main practical benefit of TLS 1.3 over older versions?
choices: It is faster -- it completes the handshake in fewer round trips and drops weak old options
It removes the need for any encryption
It works without certificates
It makes websites use more data
items.explanation: TLS 1.3 streamlines the handshake (fewer round trips, so secure pages start sooner) and keeps only strong modern options. It does not remove encryption or certificates -- those are essential -- and it is about being leaner, not using more data.

items.prompt: What does forward secrecy protect against?
choices: An attacker who recorded your encrypted traffic today and later steals the server's long-term private key -- they still cannot decrypt the old recording
An attacker who guesses your password
A website that loads too slowly
A virus on your own computer
items.explanation: Forward secrecy uses fresh, throwaway keys per session, so a private key stolen LATER cannot unlock traffic recorded earlier. It is specifically about protecting past sessions; it does not address password guessing, slow loading, or local malware.

items.prompt: Your browser shows a warning that the certificate is EXPIRED. What does that most likely mean?
choices: The certificate's valid-until date has passed -- often the site owner forgot to renew it
The website has been permanently shut down
Your internet connection is too slow
Your browser is out of date and must be reinstalled
items.explanation: An expired-certificate warning means the certificate is past its valid-until date, commonly because it was not renewed in time. It does not mean the site is gone, that your connection is slow, or that your browser is broken.

items.prompt: A SELF-SIGNED certificate triggers a warning because:
choices: It was signed only by the site itself, not by a trusted CA, so the browser cannot confirm the identity
It is encrypted too strongly for the browser to read
It belongs to a different website
It has the wrong file name
items.explanation: A self-signed certificate is vouched for by no trusted authority -- like a homemade ID card -- so the browser cannot verify who you are talking to. That is different from a NAME MISMATCH (belonging to a different site) and has nothing to do with encryption strength or file names.

items.prompt: You visit shop.example.com but the certificate is issued for example.com only. What kind of warning is this?
choices: A name mismatch -- the certificate's name does not match the site you actually visited
An expired certificate
A revoked certificate
A forward-secrecy error
items.explanation: When the certificate's name does not cover the exact site you visited, the browser reports a name mismatch, because the identity does not line up. It is not about expiry, revocation, or forward secrecy -- those are different checks.

items.prompt: What is 'defense in depth,' and where does TLS fit?
choices: Relying on multiple layers of protection rather than one; TLS secures a connection, while tools like a VPN or IPsec add further encrypted layers
Using one very strong password and nothing else
Turning off encryption to make the network simpler
Trusting any device once it is inside your home network
items.explanation: Defense in depth means stacking protections so that if one is bypassed, others still help. TLS protects a single connection; a VPN (often built with IPsec) can wrap all of a device's traffic as an additional layer. Relying on a single password, disabling encryption, or blindly trusting the local network are the opposite of this idea.

items.prompt: Should you click through a certificate warning on your bank's website?
choices: No -- the warning means the browser could not safely confirm who you are talking to, so sensitive data is at risk
Yes -- warnings are always just bugs and can be ignored
Yes -- clicking through makes the connection faster
It does not matter, because TLS protects you either way
items.explanation: A certificate warning means a safety check failed and the site's identity could not be confirmed, so on anything sensitive like banking you should not proceed. Warnings are not mere bugs, clicking through does not speed anything up, and TLS cannot protect you if you have overridden the very check that proves who you are talking to.

lessons.title: HTTP: Request/Response, Methods, Status, and State
lessons.content:
## The top of the stack: the language programs speak

You have built up a stack: **IP** gets a packet to the right computer, **TCP** delivers the bytes reliably and in order, and **ports** pick which program receives them. But once two programs are connected, what do they actually *say* to each other? That is the job of the **application layer** -- the very top of the stack.

The **application layer** is the set of **languages programs use to talk to each other** once the lower layers have set up delivery. The transport layer is like a phone line that connects two people; the application layer is the *language they speak* on the call. Different jobs use different languages: fetching a web page, sending an email, and moving a file each have their own.

## The most important one: HTTP

By far the most common application-layer language is **HTTP** (HyperText Transfer Protocol) -- the **request/response language of the web**. Here is the whole idea:

- Your **browser** is the **client** -- "the one who asks." It sends a **request**, such as "GET me the home page."
- The **web server** is the **server** -- "the one who answers." It sends back a **response**.

One request goes out; exactly one response comes back. A **response** has two parts: a **status code** (did it work?) and a **body** (the actual content -- the page, the image, the data).

## The restaurant analogy

Think of ordering at a restaurant. **You** are the client: you make a **request** from the menu. The **kitchen** is the server: it **responds** with your dish. The waiter's reply is like a **status code** -- "here you go" (success), "we're out of that" (your item doesn't exist), or "there's a problem in the kitchen" (the server broke). Same pattern as the web: ask, then receive an answer plus a short status.

## Naming what you want: the URL

How does the browser say *what* it wants? With a **URL** (Uniform Resource Locator) -- a web address like `https://example.com/photos/cat.jpg`. It names the server (`example.com`) and the exact item on it (`/photos/cat.jpg`). You type or click a URL; the browser turns it into an HTTP request.

## A couple of request types

Requests carry an **intent**, called a **method**. The two you will meet first:

- **GET** -- "give me this" (read something, like loading a page). GET just *fetches*; it doesn't change anything on the server.
- **POST** -- "here is some data, take it" (submit something, like sending a form or posting a comment).

(There are a few more, like **PUT** to replace an item and **DELETE** to remove one, but GET and POST are the everyday pair.)

## Recap

The application layer is the **program-to-program language** that sits on top of TCP/IP. The most important one is **HTTP**, the web's **request/response** pattern: a **client** (browser) sends a request naming a **URL**; a **server** sends back a **status code** plus a **body**. Methods like **GET** (read) and **POST** (submit) say what the request is for.

items.prompt: What is the application layer?
choices: The set of languages programs use to talk to each other once the lower layers have set up delivery
The physical cable that carries the signal
The part of the network that assigns IP addresses
A backup copy of the data in case the connection drops
items.explanation: The application layer sits at the TOP of the stack: once IP and TCP have set up delivery, it is the language two programs actually speak (HTTP for the web, others for email or files). The cable is the physical layer, IP addressing is the network layer, and 'a backup copy' is not a layer at all.

items.prompt: What is HTTP, in plain terms?
choices: The request/response language of the web: a client asks for something and a server answers
A way to encrypt files before saving them to disk
The protocol that assigns each computer its IP address
A type of network cable used between routers
items.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the web's request/response language: the browser (client) sends a request and the server sends back a response. It is not encryption (that is TLS), not address assignment (that is DHCP), and not hardware.

items.prompt: In a web exchange, which side is the CLIENT and which is the SERVER?
choices: The browser is the client (it asks); the web server is the server (it answers)
The browser is the server; the web server is the client
Both are clients; there is no server in HTTP
Whichever computer is faster is always the server
items.explanation: The client is 'the one who asks' -- your browser sending the request. The server is 'the one who answers' -- the machine sending back the page. The roles are about who requests and who responds, not about speed, and they are not interchangeable in a single exchange.

items.prompt: An HTTP response is made of which two parts?
choices: A status code (did it work?) and a body (the actual content)
A username and a password
An IP address and a port number
A request method and a URL
items.explanation: A response carries a status code that says whether the request succeeded, plus a body holding the content (the page, image, or data). Username/password and IP/port belong to other parts of networking; a method and URL are parts of the REQUEST, not the response.

items.prompt: In the restaurant analogy for HTTP, what plays the role of the SERVER?
choices: The kitchen, which responds to your order with a dish
The menu you read
The front door of the restaurant
The money you pay at the end
items.explanation: You (the client) make a request from the menu; the kitchen (the server) responds with your dish. The menu is more like the list of things you can request, and the door and the bill are not part of the request/response idea.

items.prompt: You click a link and your browser loads a page WITHOUT changing anything on the server. Which method is this?
choices: GET -- 'give me this' (read something)
POST -- 'here is data, take it' (submit something)
DELETE -- remove an item
PUT -- replace an item
items.explanation: GET means 'fetch this' and does not change anything on the server -- exactly what loading a page is. POST submits data (like a form), DELETE removes something, and PUT replaces something; all three are meant to change server state, unlike a plain page load.

items.prompt: You fill in a sign-up form and click Submit, sending your data to the server. Which method fits best?
choices: POST -- it submits data to the server
GET -- it only reads, sending no data to change anything
HEAD -- it asks for just the headers
There is no method for sending form data
items.explanation: POST is the method for submitting data (creating an account, posting a comment). GET is for reading and is not meant to carry data that changes the server. HTTP absolutely supports sending form data -- that is what POST is for.

items.prompt: In the address https://example.com/photos/cat.jpg , what is this whole string called and what does it do?
choices: A URL -- it names the server (example.com) and the exact item (/photos/cat.jpg) to request
A status code -- it reports whether the request worked
A cookie -- it stores your login
A port number -- it picks which program receives the data
items.explanation: A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is the web address that names both the server and the specific resource on it; the browser turns it into an HTTP request. A status code is part of the response, a cookie stores session info, and a port number is a lower-layer detail -- none of those is the address itself.

items.prompt: How many responses come back for a single HTTP request?
choices: Exactly one response per request
None -- HTTP requests are one-way and never answered
Always two: one to confirm and one with the data
As many as the server feels like sending, with no fixed number
items.explanation: HTTP is a clean request/response pair: one request, exactly one response. It is not fire-and-forget (you do get an answer), and the basic model is not 'confirm then data' or an open-ended stream -- that single response carries the status and the body together.

items.prompt: Why does HTTP rely on the transport layer (TCP) underneath it?
choices: TCP reliably delivers the bytes in order; HTTP is just the language spoken over that connection
TCP encrypts the page so no one can read it
TCP chooses which web page the user wants
HTTP does not use the transport layer at all
items.explanation: The application layer sits on top of transport: TCP handles reliable, in-order delivery, and HTTP is the conversation carried over it. TCP does not encrypt (that is TLS) and does not decide content; and HTTP definitely depends on a transport layer to move its messages.

lessons.title: Status Codes, Headers, and HTTPS
lessons.content:
## Reading the server's answer: status codes

Every HTTP response starts with a **status code** -- a three-digit number that tells you, at a glance, how the request went. You don't need to memorize hundreds of them. The trick is to **read the first digit**, which puts the code into one of a few **families**:

- **2xx -- Success.** It worked. The classic is **200 OK**: "here is what you asked for."
- **3xx -- Redirect.** "What you want lives somewhere else; go there." Example: **301 Moved Permanently**.
- **4xx -- Your mistake (the client's).** You asked for something wrong or not allowed. The famous one is **404 Not Found**: "there is no such page."
- **5xx -- The server's fault.** Your request was fine, but the server broke trying to answer. The classic is **500 Internal Server Error**.

So **200 vs 404 vs 500** tells a quick story: 200 = it worked; 404 = *you* asked for something that isn't there; 500 = *the server* failed. (There is also a rare 1xx "still working" family, but you'll mostly see 2/3/4/5.)

## Headers: extra info attached to the message

Besides the status and the body, both requests and responses carry **headers** -- lines of **extra information** *about* the message. Think of them as notes on the envelope rather than the letter inside. A few you'll hear about:

- **Content-Type** -- what kind of thing the body is. `text/html` means a web page; `image/png` means a picture; `application/json` means data. This is how your browser knows whether to draw a page or show an image.
- **Content-Length** -- how big the body is.
- **Cookie / Set-Cookie** -- small notes used to remember you between requests (the next lesson digs into these).

You rarely write headers by hand; the browser and server add them automatically. The point to remember is *headers = extra info about the message*.

## HTTPS = HTTP over TLS

You met **TLS** last lesson: the secure tunnel that scrambles data and proves you're talking to the real site. **HTTPS is simply HTTP running inside that TLS tunnel** -- the **S** stands for **Secure**. Nothing about the requests and responses changes; the browser still sends `GET` and gets back `200 OK` plus a body. The whole conversation is just wrapped so a snoop can't read or change it. The **padlock** icon in the address bar means "this page came over HTTPS."

Picture the stack: **HTTP** sits on **TLS**, which sits on **TCP**, which sits on **IP**. Plain `http://` skips the TLS box; secure `https://` includes it. Today almost all real websites use HTTPS.

## Recap

A **status code**'s first digit gives the family: **2xx** success, **3xx** redirect, **4xx** your mistake, **5xx** server error -- with **200 / 301 / 404 / 500** as the everyday examples. **Headers** carry extra info about a message, like **Content-Type** (is the body a page, an image, or data?). And **HTTPS is just HTTP inside a TLS tunnel**, shown by the padlock.

items.prompt: What is the quickest way to understand an HTTP status code?
choices: Read the first digit -- it puts the code into a family (2xx, 3xx, 4xx, 5xx)
Read the last digit -- it tells you the exact error
Add the three digits together
Look up every code individually; there is no pattern
items.explanation: The first digit groups codes into families: 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error. That single digit tells you most of the story. The other options miss the built-in pattern that makes status codes easy to read.

items.prompt: What does a 200 status code mean?
choices: Success -- the request worked and here is what you asked for
The page was not found
The server crashed while handling the request
You must log in first
items.explanation: 200 OK is the most common success (2xx) code: the request worked and the body holds the result. 'Not found' is 404, a server crash is 500, and 'must log in' is a 401 -- all different families from a 2xx success.

items.prompt: You request a page that does not exist. Which status code do you get, and whose 'mistake' is it?
choices: 404 Not Found -- a 4xx, meaning the client asked for something that isn't there
500 Internal Server Error -- a 5xx, meaning the server failed
200 OK -- a 2xx, meaning success
301 Moved Permanently -- a 3xx redirect
items.explanation: 404 is a 4xx 'client error': you asked for a resource that doesn't exist. A 500 would mean the server itself broke, 200 means it worked, and 301 means the resource moved elsewhere. The key contrast: 4xx points at the request, 5xx points at the server.

items.prompt: A request is perfectly valid, but the server itself breaks while handling it. Which family of status code is that?
choices: 5xx -- a server error (e.g., 500)
4xx -- a client error (e.g., 404)
2xx -- success
3xx -- redirect
items.explanation: 5xx codes mean the server failed even though the request was fine -- 500 Internal Server Error is the classic. 4xx blames the request, 2xx is success, and 3xx sends you elsewhere. The 4xx-vs-5xx split is exactly 'your fault vs the server's fault'.

items.prompt: What does a 3xx status code (like 301) tell the browser to do?
choices: The resource lives somewhere else -- go to the new location
The request succeeded and here is the content
The page does not exist
The server is overloaded and crashed
items.explanation: 3xx is the redirect family: '301 Moved Permanently' means the page has a new address and the browser should go there. Success is 2xx, a missing page is 404 (4xx), and a crash is 500 (5xx).

items.prompt: What are HTTP headers?
choices: Lines of extra information about the message -- like notes on the envelope, separate from the body
The main content of the page itself
The IP address the packet is going to
The encryption keys used by TLS
items.explanation: Headers carry extra info ABOUT the message (its type, size, cookies), separate from the body, which is the actual content. They are not the page content itself, not the IP address (a lower layer), and not TLS keys.

items.prompt: The Content-Type header on a response says application/json. What does that tell the browser?
choices: The body is data in JSON format, not a web page or an image
The body is a PNG image
The connection is encrypted with TLS
The request failed with an error
items.explanation: Content-Type announces what kind of thing the body is; application/json means the body is JSON data. text/html would be a web page and image/png would be a picture. Content-Type is about the body's format, not about encryption or success.

items.prompt: What exactly is HTTPS?
choices: HTTP running inside a TLS tunnel -- the same requests/responses, just encrypted and verified
A completely different protocol that replaces HTTP with new commands
HTTP that has been compressed to load faster
A protocol only used for sending email
items.explanation: HTTPS is just HTTP carried over TLS: the same GET/200/body, wrapped so a snoop can't read or change it -- that is the 'S' for Secure, shown by the padlock. It does not change HTTP's commands, it is not merely compression, and it is for the web, not email.

items.prompt: In the layer stack, what sits directly between HTTP and TCP to make HTTPS?
choices: TLS -- the secure tunnel that scrambles and verifies the data
IP -- the layer that addresses the packet
DNS -- the system that turns names into IP addresses
Nothing -- HTTPS connects HTTP straight to TCP with no extra layer
items.explanation: HTTPS inserts TLS between HTTP and TCP: HTTP on TLS on TCP on IP. IP is further down the stack, DNS is a separate lookup service, and 'nothing' describes plain HTTP -- the TLS box is exactly what makes it HTTPS.

items.prompt: Which header would tell you how many bytes are in the response body?
choices: Content-Length
Content-Type
Set-Cookie
Status-Code
items.explanation: Content-Length reports the size of the body in bytes. Content-Type reports the KIND of body, Set-Cookie stores a small note for later requests, and 'Status-Code' is not a header -- the status code is the number on the response's first line.

lessons.title: Statelessness, Cookies, and Other Protocols
lessons.content:
## A surprising fact: the web has no memory

Here is something odd about HTTP: it is **stateless**. That means **each request stands alone** -- by itself, the server does not remember anything about your previous requests. Every request arrives like a stranger walking up for the first time.

That sounds like a problem. If the server forgets you after every click, how does a website keep you **logged in** as you move from page to page? You don't re-type your password on every page, so something must be carrying your identity along.

## Cookies and sessions: how the web remembers you

The fix is a **cookie**: a small note the server gives your browser to hold and show back later. The flow is simple:

1. You log in. The server replies with a header: **`Set-Cookie: id=A7X9`** -- "here is your ticket."
2. Your browser stores that ticket and **automatically sends it back** on every later request: **`Cookie: id=A7X9`**.
3. The server sees the ticket and thinks "ah, that's you -- welcome back," without asking for your password again.

The matching record on the server's side is your **session** -- the server's memory of who `A7X9` is and what you're doing. It's exactly like a **coat-check ticket**: the coat (your session) stays with the attendant; the little numbered stub (the cookie) is what you carry to claim it. So even though HTTP itself forgets, cookies + sessions layer memory on top.

## HTTP is just one application protocol

HTTP rules the web, but it's only one language at the application layer. Many everyday tasks have their own. A quick tour, grouped by job:

- **Names to IP addresses -- DNS.** You met **DNS** (Domain Name System) earlier: it turns a name like `example.com` into an IP address so the connection can be made. Almost every web action starts with a DNS lookup.
- **Email -- SMTP, IMAP, POP.** **SMTP** (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is for **sending** mail. To **read** mail you've received, your app uses **IMAP** (keeps mail synced on the server) or the older **POP** (typically downloads it). Memory hook: **S**MTP to **S**end.
- **Files -- FTP and SFTP.** **FTP** (File Transfer Protocol) moves files between machines; **SFTP** is the secure version (encrypted).
- **Remote control -- SSH.** **SSH** (Secure Shell) gives you an encrypted command-line login to another computer -- like safely sitting at its keyboard from far away.
- **Live, two-way -- WebSocket.** Plain HTTP is one-shot (ask, answer, done). A **WebSocket** keeps a connection open so **both sides can send messages anytime** -- perfect for chat, multiplayer games, and live dashboards.
- **Tiny devices -- MQTT.** **MQTT** is a lightweight protocol for **IoT** (Internet of Things -- small connected gadgets like sensors). It uses a **publish/subscribe** style: devices publish readings to a central hub, and whoever is interested subscribes -- efficient for low-power, low-bandwidth gadgets.

Don't memorize every detail. The skill is recognizing **which protocol does which job**, and that HTTP is the famous one among many.

## Recap

HTTP is **stateless** -- each request stands alone -- so the web uses **cookies** (a ticket your browser shows back) tied to a server-side **session** to remember you, especially for staying logged in. Beyond HTTP, other application protocols handle other jobs: **DNS** (names to IPs), **SMTP/IMAP/POP** (email send/read), **FTP/SFTP** (files), **SSH** (secure remote login), **WebSocket** (live two-way), and **MQTT** (tiny IoT devices).

items.prompt: What does it mean that HTTP is 'stateless'?
choices: Each request stands alone; by itself the server does not remember your previous requests
The server permanently remembers everything every client has ever done
HTTP cannot carry any data at all
The connection can never be encrypted
items.explanation: Stateless means every request is independent -- on its own, the server keeps no memory of earlier requests from you. That is the opposite of 'remembers everything', and it has nothing to do with whether HTTP carries data or can be encrypted.

items.prompt: If HTTP forgets you after each request, how does a website keep you logged in across pages?
choices: A cookie: the browser stores a small ticket and sends it back on every later request
The server memorizes your face
You must re-type your password on every single page
It keeps one TCP connection open forever for you alone
items.explanation: A cookie is the standard fix: the server sends a ticket (Set-Cookie) and the browser returns it (Cookie) on later requests, so the server recognizes you without a fresh login. You do NOT re-type your password each page, and the trick is the cookie, not a single eternal connection.

items.prompt: In the cookie flow, which header does the SERVER use to first hand the browser its ticket?
choices: Set-Cookie
Cookie
Content-Type
User-Agent
